facts.text: Benvenuto Cellini lived from 1500 to 1571.
Cellini was born in Florence.
Cellini died at Florence on May 13, 1571.
Cellini was buried in The Church of the Annunziata in Florence.
Cellini was a worker in gold and silver.
Cellini was a sculptor.
Cellini was a musician.
Cellini was a master of narrative.
Cellini was a murderer.
Cellini was a braggart.
Cellini was insolent, sensual, inordinately proud and passionate.
Cellini created a work called 'Perseus'.
Cellini dictated his autobiography to a boy of fourteen.
Cellini's autobiography was composed between 1558 and 1566.
Cellini's autobiography covers the period from his birth in 1500 to 1562.
Cellini married Piera de Salvadore Parigi in 1565.
Piera de Salvadore Parigi was a servant who had nursed Cellini when he was sick.
Cellini showed tenderness in the care of his children and earlier of his sister and nieces.
Cellini knew Michelangelo and adored him.
Cellini fought in the Sack of Rome.
Cellini was called Benvenuto Cellini, son of Maestro Giovanni.
Cellini's mother was Madonna Elisabetta, daughter to Stefano Granacci.
Both of Cellini's parents were citizens of Florence.
Cellini's ancestors dwelt in Val d'Ambra.
Cellini's ancestors owned large estates in Val d'Ambra.
Cellini's ancestors were men devoted to arms and of notable bravery.
Cristofano Cellini was sent to Florence to settle a family feud.
Andrea Cellini was very well versed in architecture.
Giovanni Cellini, Benvenuto's father, paid more attention to architecture than his brothers.
Giovanni Cellini mastered drawing and turned his mind to music.
Giovanni Cellini played excellently on the viol and the flute.
Giovanni Cellini was a person of studious habits who left his home but seldom.
Giovanni Cellini married Elisabetta Granacci.
Giovanni and Elisabetta enjoyed their youth and wedded love through eighteen years.
Giovanni and Elisabetta greatly desired to be blessed with children.
Elisabetta miscarried of two boys through the unskilfulness of the doctors.
Elisabetta gave birth to a girl named Cosa.
Benvenuto Cellini was born on a night of All Saints, following the feast-day, at half-past four precisely, in the year 1500.
Benvenuto Cellini was an unexpected male child when his parents were expecting a girl.
Giovanni Cellini was a true philosopher.
Giovanni Cellini named his son Benvenuto, meaning 'Welcome'.
Cellini believed that men of merit and good repute should write the tale of their life with their own hand.
Cellini held that he had done many things of merit.
Cellini's repute was great in his day.
Cellini had some misrepresentations and suppressions of damaging facts in his autobiography.
Cellini had a passion for proving himself in the right.
Cellini was travelling beyond the term of fifty-eight years when he began writing his autobiography.
Cellini was more free from adversities at fifty-eight than at any previous period of his career.
Cellini enjoyed greater content of soul and health of body at fifty-eight than in bygone years.
Cellini was born humble.
Cellini took more pride in being born humble and laying an honourable foundation for his family than in being born of great lineage.
There are Cellinis of Cellini's stock in Ravenna.
There are Cellinis in Pisa.
Cellini discovered Cellinis in many parts of Christendom.
Luca Cellini, a beardless youth, fought with Francesco da Vicorati in single combat.
Luca Cellini overcame and slew Francesco da Vicorati with sword in hand.
Giovanni Cellini asked Elisabetta Granacci in marriage.
Andrea Cellini and Stefano Granacci were well acquainted through their close neighbourhood.
Andrea Cellini boasted that Giovanni was the stoutest youth of Florence and of all Italy.
Giovanni Cellini sought and loved Elisabetta and not her money.
Giovanni Cellini took his wife and never asked for other portion with her.
Cellini was born on a night of All Saints in the year 1500 at half-past four.
Cellini was born male, contrary to his parents' expectation of a female child.
Cellini's father Giovanni named him Benvenuto, meaning 'Welcome.'
Cellini's grandfather Andrea Cellini was alive when Cellini was about three years old and had passed his hundredth year.
As a young child, Cellini picked up a scorpion with his bare hands.
Cellini's father cut off the scorpion's tail and mouths to save Cellini from the venomous animal.
When Cellini was about five years old, his father showed him a salamander in a fire.
Cellini's father struck him on the ears to make him remember the salamander.
Cellini had an inexpressible dislike for playing the flute and singing as a child.
Cellini's father was skilled at fashioning organs, spinets, viols, lutes, and harps.
Cellini's father was an engineer with skill in making instruments for lowering bridges and working mills.
Cellini's father was the first to work well in ivory.
Cellini's father fell in love with the woman who became Cellini's mother.
Cellini's father was entreated by the fifers of the Signory to play in their company.
Lorenzo de' Medici and Pietro his son perceived that Cellini's father was neglecting his engineering talent and art.
Lorenzo de' Medici and Pietro had Cellini's father removed from his post as a fifer.
Cellini's father took his removal from the post very ill and felt greatly offended.
Cellini's father fashioned a mirror about a cubit in diameter out of bone and ivory in the form of a wheel.
The mirror Cellini's father made featured the Seven Virtues carved in ivory and black bone.
Cellini's father inscribed a Latin legend on his mirror: 'Rota sum: semper, quoquo me verto, stat Virtus.'
Cellini's father obtained his place again among the fifers after some time.
Musicians of the Signory in those days were members of the most honourable trades.
Cellini's father's chief desire was that Cellini should become a great performer on the flute.
Cellini felt discontented when his father spoke to him about becoming a flute player.
Cellini's father was a devoted servant and attached friend of the house of Medici.
When Piero Medici was banished, he entrusted Cellini's father with many affairs of great importance.
Piero Soderini employed Cellini's father in many matters of great importance as an engineer.
Cellini performed on the flute as a child before the Signory in the palace.
A beadle used to carry young Cellini upon his shoulders when he performed.
Soderini took much pleasure in making Cellini chatter and gave him comfits.
Soderini advised Cellini's father to teach Cellini arts other than music.
The Medici returned to Florence after their exile.
The Cardinal, who later became Pope Leo X, received Cellini's father very kindly upon the Medici's return.
During the Medici's exile, the balls on the palace scutcheon were erased and a red cross was painted over them.
Cellini's father wrote four prophetic verses under the arms of the Medici when they were uncovered.
Pope Julius II died a few days after Cellini's father's epigram was read by all Florence.
The Cardinal de' Medici was elected Pope against everybody's expectation and reigned as Leo X.
Cellini's father sent his four prophetic verses to Pope Leo X.
Pope Leo X sent word for Cellini's father to come to Rome, saying it would be to his advantage.
Cellini's father had no will to go to Rome.
Jacopo Salviati took Cellini's father's place in the palace upon his election as Gonfalonier.
Cellini commenced goldsmith work as a result of his father losing his position.
Cellini entreated his father to let him draw a certain fixed number of hours in the day.
Cellini told his father that the art of music seemed too base in comparison with what he had in his own mind.
Cellini's father placed him in the workshop of Michel Agnolo, a goldsmith and father of Bandinello.
Michel Agnolo was from Pinzi di Monte and was the son of a charcoal-seller.
Cellini's father took him away from Michel Agnolo after some days, unable to live without having him always under his eyes.
Cellini remained at music until he reached the age of fifteen against his will.
At age fifteen, Cellini put himself to the goldsmith's trade with a man called Antonio, son of Sandro, known as Marcone the goldsmith.
Marcone was a most excellent craftsman and a very good fellow, high-spirited and frank in all his ways.
Cellini's father would not let Marcone give Cellini wages like the other apprentices.
Marcone took marvellous delight in Cellini's performances.
Marcone had an only son, a bastard, to whom he often gave his orders to spare Cellini.
In a few months, Cellini caught up with the best young craftsmen in the goldsmith business.
Cellini did not neglect to gratify his father by playing the flute or cornet from time to time.
Cellini's father wept with deep-drawn sighs of satisfaction each time he heard Cellini play.
Cellini had a brother younger by two years with extreme boldness and fierce temper.
Cellini's brother became one of the great soldiers in the school of Giovannino de' Medici, father of Duke Cosimo.
Cellini's brother was about fourteen when he dueled with a young fellow of twenty.
Cellini's brother dealt valiantly in his duel and badly wounded his opponent.
A stone from a sling hit Cellini's brother in the head during the duel.
Cellini was present at his brother's duel alone and without arms.
Cellini seized his brother's sword and stood in front of him during the fight.
Cellini bore the brunt of several rapiers and a shower of stones to protect his brother.
Brave soldiers from the gate San Gallo rescued Cellini from the raging crowd.
The soldiers marvelled at finding such valour in so young a boy as Cellini.
Cellini carried his brother home, and it was only with great difficulty that his brother came to himself again.
The Eight condemned Cellini's brother's adversaries and banished them for a term of years.
The Eight sent Cellini and his brother into exile for six months at a distance of ten miles from Florence.
Cellini told his brother to come along with him into exile.
Cellini and his brother took leave of their poor father, who blessed them instead of giving them money.
Cellini went to Siena, wishing to look up a man called Maestro Francesco Castoro.
Cellini had previously run away from his father and stayed with Maestro Francesco Castoro, working at the goldsmith's trade.
Cellini ran up and seized his brother's sword when his brother was attacked.
Cellini stood in front of his brother bearing the brunt of several rapiers and a shower of stones.
Cellini never left his brother's side until soldiers from the gate San Gallo rescued him.
Soldiers marveled at finding such valor in so young a boy.
Cellini and his brother were sent into exile for six months at a distance of ten miles from Florence.
Cellini's father blessed them instead of giving them money because he had none.
Cellini went to Siena to look up Maestro Francesco Castoro.
Cellini had previously run away from his father and stayed with Francesco Castoro while working at the goldsmith's trade.
Francesco Castoro recognized Cellini at once and gave him work to do.
Francesco Castoro placed a house at Cellini's disposal for the whole time of his sojourn in Siena.
Cellini's brother had acquired the rudiments of Latin.
Cellini's brother was too young to relish virtuous employment and passed his time in dissipation.
The Cardinal de' Medici had Cellini and his father recalled to Florence.
A pupil of Cellini's father suggested to the Cardinal that Cellini should be sent to Bologna to learn music.
The music master in Bologna was named Antonio.
The Cardinal said he would give Cellini letters of recommendation and support if sent to Bologna.
Cellini's father sent him to Bologna with good grace.
Cellini put himself under Maestro Ercole del Piffero in Bologna.
Cellini began to earn something by his goldsmith trade in Bologna.
Cellini went every day to take his music lesson in Bologna.
Cellini made considerable progress in music in a few weeks.
Cellini made greater progress in the goldsmith trade than in music.
The Cardinal gave Cellini no assistance in Bologna.
Cellini went to live with a Bolognese illuminator called Scipione Cavalletti.
Cellini devoted himself to drawing and working for Graziadio, a Jew.
Cellini earned considerably working for Graziadio.
Cellini returned to Florence after six months in Bologna.
Pierino was greatly mortified by Cellini's return to Florence.
Cellini played the cornet and flute with Girolamo, Pierino's brother.
Girolamo was several years younger than Pierino and a very worthy young man.
Cellini's father exclaimed he would make Cellini a marvellous musician against the will of all.
Pierino told Cellini's father that Benvenuto would get more honor and profit from the goldsmiths trade than from music.
Cellini agreed with Pierino's opinion about the goldsmith trade.
Cellini's father became angry at Pierino for putting obstacles in the way of his wish.
Cellini's father accused Pierino of having him ousted from his place at the palace.
Cellini's father prophesied that Pierino would be ruined in only a few weeks.
Pierino responded that Cellini's father had gone mad in his old age.
Pierino said Cellini's father had squandered all his property without reflecting that his children needed it.
Pierino said he intended to leave his children so much that they could succor Cellini's father's children.
Cellini's father prophesied that Pierino's children would be mad and paupers and would cringe for alms to his virtuous and wealthy sons.
Cellini took his father's part in the dispute with Pierino.
Cellini told his father he was ready to take vengeance for the insults heaped on him by Pierino.
Cellini offered vengeance provided his father permit him to give himself up to the art of design.
Cellini's father said he too in his time was a good draughtsman.
Cellini's father asked Cellini to sometimes play the flute and cornet for recreation and for love of him.
Cellini promised to play the flute and cornet willingly for his father's sake.
Cellini's father said that excellent parts would be the greatest vengeance for the insults of his enemies.
Not a whole month after the scene with Pierino, Pierino was building a vault in a house on the Via dello Studio.
Pierino fell through a floor while standing above the vault he was making.
Stones and bricks from the vault broke both of Pierino's legs.
Pierino's friends who were with him remained on the border of the broken vault and took no harm.
Pierino's friends were astounded by the prophecy that had just been repeated.
Cellini's father took his sword and went to see Pierino after hearing of the accident.
Cellini's father told Pierino he had warned him of this mischance a short time before.
Pierino's father was called Niccolaio da Volterra, a trumpeter of the Signory.
Pierino died of the illness resulting from his accident.
Pierino left a wife of bad character and one son.
Pierino's son came to Cellini in Rome to beg for alms after some years.
Cellini gave Pierino's son something because of his charitable nature.
Cellini recalled with tears the happy state Pierino held when his father spoke the prophecy.
Cellini worked as a goldsmith and was able to assist his good father.
Cellini's brother Cecchino had been instructed in the rudiments of Latin letters.
Cellini's father wished to make Cellini a great musician and composer.
Cellini's father wished to make Cecchino a great and learned jurist.
Cellini's natural inclinations directed him to the arts of design.
Cecchino's natural inclinations directed him to the profession of arms.
Cecchino was returning from his first lesson in the school of Giovannino de' Medici.
Cecchino sought out their sisters for proper clothes when he reached home.
Cellini's sisters gave Cecchino a cloak and doublet of Cellini's, both new and of good quality.
Cellini had bought those handsome clothes out of his own savings.
Cellini found he had been cheated and his clothes taken from him.
Cellini asked his father why he suffered so great a wrong to be done to him.
Cellini's father replied that Cellini was his good son.
Cellini's father said the other son, whom he thought to have lost, had been found again.
Cellini's father said it was a duty and precept from God that he who hath should give to him who hath not.
Cellini's father said God would increase Cellini in all good things.
Cellini retorted on his poor afflicted parent like a youth without experience.
Cellini took the miserable remnants of his clothes and money and went toward a gate of the city.
Cellini did not know which gate would start him on the road to Rome.
Cellini arrived at Lucca and from Lucca reached Pisa.
Cellini was about sixteen years of age when he came to Pisa.
Cellini stopped near the middle bridge by the Fish-stone at a goldsmith's shop in Pisa.
Cellini began attentively to watch what the goldsmith master was about.
Cellini told the goldsmith he worked a little in the same trade.
The goldsmith bade Cellini come into his shop and at once gave him work to do.
The goldsmith said Cellini's good appearance made him believe he was a decent honest youth.
The goldsmith told out gold, silver, and gems to Cellini.
The goldsmith took Cellini to his house in the evening after the first day's work.
The goldsmith dwelt respectably with his handsome wife and children.
Cellini wrote to his father that he was sojourning with Maestro Ulivieri della Chiostra.
Cellini told his father he was working with Ulivieri at many good things of beauty and importance.
Cellini told his father he was bent on learning and hoped to bring him profit and honor.
Cellini's father answered the letter at once.
Cellini's father said the love he bore Cellini was so great that he would have set off for him if not for the honor of their family.
Cellini's father said it seemed like being without the light of his eyes when he did not see Cellini daily.
Cellini's father said he would complete the training of his household up to virtuous honesty.
Cellini's father told him to acquire excellence in his art.
Cellini's father gave Cellini four simple words: In whatever house you be, Steal not, and live honestly.
The letter from Cellini's father fell into the hands of Cellini's master Ulivieri.
Ulivieri read the letter unknown to Cellini.
Ulivieri afterwards avowed that he had read the letter.
Ulivieri said Cellini's good looks did not deceive him.
Ulivieri said the letter proved Cellini's father to be a man of notable honesty and worth.
Ulivieri told Cellini to consider himself at home and as though in his own father's house.
While at Pisa, Cellini went to see the Campo Santo.
Cellini found many beautiful fragments of antiquity in the Campo Santo, including marble sarcophagi.
Cellini saw many antique objects in other parts of Pisa.
Cellini diligently studied antique objects whenever he had days or hours free from workshop labor.
Cellini's master took pleasure in visiting him in the little room allotted to him.
Cellini's master observed that he spent all his time in studious occupations.
Cellini's master began to love him like a father.
Cellini made great progress in the one year he stayed at Pisa.
Cellini completed several fine and valuable things in gold and silver at Pisa.
Cellini's accomplishments inspired him with a resolute ambition to advance in his art.
Cellini's father kept writing piteous entreaties that he should return.
Cellini's father bade him in every letter not to lose the music he had taught him.
Cellini suddenly gave up all wish to go back to his father.
Cellini hated the accursed music.
Cellini felt as though he were in paradise the whole year he stayed at Pisa.
Cellini never played the flute during the year he stayed at Pisa.
At the end of the year, Cellini's master Ulivieri had occasion to go to Florence to sell gold and silver sweepings.
The bad air of Pisa had given Cellini a touch of fever.
Cellini went back to Florence with the fever hanging still about him in his master's company.
Cellini's father received Ulivieri most affectionately in Florence.
Cellini's father lovingly prayed Ulivieri not to insist on taking Cellini back to Pisa.
Cellini was ill about two months after returning to Florence.
Cellini's father had him most kindly treated and cured.
Cellini's father said it seemed to him a thousand years till Cellini got well again.
Cellini's father wanted to hear Cellini play a little.
Cellini's father had some acquaintance with medicine and Latin learning.
Cellini's father felt Cellini's pulse change when he approached the topic of music.
Cellini's father was often dismayed and left Cellini's side in tears.
Cellini perceived how greatly his father was disappointed.
Cellini bade one of his sisters bring him a flute.
The fever never left Cellini, but the flute was so easy that it did not hurt him to play upon it.
Cellini played the flute with such dexterity of hand and tongue.
Cellini's father came suddenly upon him playing and blessed him a thousand times.
Cellini's father exclaimed that Cellini had made great progress while away from him.
Cellini's father begged him to go forwards and not to sacrifice so fine an accomplishment.
When Cellini had recovered his health, he returned to his old friend Marcone, the worthy goldsmith.
Marcone put Cellini in the way of earning money.
Cellini helped his father and their household with the money he earned.
A sculptor named Piero Torrigiani came to Florence.
Piero Torrigiani arrived from England, where he had resided many years.
Piero Torrigiani was intimate with Cellini's master.
Piero Torrigiani daily visited Cellini's master's house.
Cellini's father was often dismayed and left his side in tears when discussing a certain topic.
Cellini played the flute despite having a fever.
Cellini's father blessed him and encouraged him to continue developing his musical accomplishment.
Cellini returned to work with the goldsmith Marcone after recovering his health.
Cellini used money earned from goldsmithing to help his father and household.
A sculptor named Piero Torrigiani came to Florence from England.
Torrigiani was intimate with Cellini's master and visited his house daily.
Torrigiani told Cellini that his method of working and designs were worthy of a sculptor rather than a goldsmith.
Torrigiani had a splendid person and arrogant spirit.
Torrigiani had a habit of knitting his brows.
Torrigiani spoke frequently about his feats among the English.
Cellini had made a drawing from a cartoon by Michelangelo.
Michelangelo's cartoon was his first masterpiece.
Michelangelo and Leonardo da Vinci each made a cartoon for the council-hall in the palace of the Signory.
Both cartoons represented the taking of Pisa by the Florentines.
Leonardo depicted a battle of horses with the capture of standards.
Michelangelo portrayed foot-soldiers bathing in the Arno at the moment an alarm is sounded.
The two cartoons stood in the palace of the Medici and the hall of the Pope.
Michelangelo finished the chapel of Pope Julius but never rose to the same pitch of power as in his first studies.
Torrigiani and Michelangelo used to go to the Church of the Carmine to learn drawing from Masaccio's chapel.
Torrigiani punched Michelangelo in the nose when they were boys.
Cellini developed hatred for Torrigiani because of his treatment of Michelangelo.
Cellini studied the noble manner of Michelangelo and never deviated from it.
Cellini contracted a close friendship with Francesco, son of Filippo and grandson of Fra Lippo Lippi.
Cellini and Francesco never stayed apart day or night during their friendship.
Francesco's house contained fine studies and books of drawings by his father from the best antiquities of Rome.
Cellini fashioned a silver bas-relief the size of a little child's hand for a belt clasp.
Cellini carved a knot of leaves in the antique style with figures of children and masks on the bas-relief.
Cellini made the bas-relief in the workshop of Francesco Salimbene.
The goldsmiths praised Cellini as the best young craftsman of their art after seeing the bas-relief.
Giovan Battista, surnamed Il Tasso, was a wood-carver of Cellini's own age.
Tasso proposed to Cellini that they go to Rome together.
Cellini was angry with his father over the music.
Cellini had money in his pockets to bring both himself and Tasso to Rome.
Cellini and Tasso reached the gate San Piero Gattolini without noticing they had arrived there.
Cellini and Tasso made an agreement not to think about their families until reaching Rome.
Cellini and Tasso tied their aprons behind their backs and traveled to Siena.
Tasso hurt his feet and asked Cellini for money to return to Florence at Siena.
Cellini hired a horse and continued toward Rome.
Tasso followed Cellini slowly and at a great distance.
Cellini waited for Tasso at the gate and took him on the crupper of his horse.
Cellini and Tasso traveled to Rome singing and laughing.
Cellini was nineteen years old when he reached Rome.
The century was also nineteen years old when Cellini reached Rome.
Cellini put himself under a master known as Il Firenzuola in Rome.
Il Firenzuola's real name was Giovanni and he came from Firenzuola in Lombardy.
Il Firenzuola was a most able craftsman in large vases and big plate.
Cellini showed Il Firenzuola part of the model for the clasp he had made in Florence.
Il Firenzuola praised Cellini's work and criticized his journeyman Giannotto Giannotti.
Cellini recognized Giannotto as a former intimate comrade from Florence.
Giannotto claimed not to recognize Cellini after Il Firenzuola criticized him.
Cellini got angry at Giannotto's denial of their friendship.
Cellini told Giannotto that his hands would declare what sort of fellow he was.
Il Firenzuola was a man of hot spirit and brave.
Il Firenzuola defended Cellini against Giannotto's treatment.
Il Firenzuola gave Cellini a fine piece of silver plate to work on for a cardinal.
The silver plate was a little oblong box copied from the porphyry sarcophagus before the door of the Rotonda.
Cellini enriched the box with elegant masks of his invention.
Il Firenzuola showed the box through the art and boasted about it.
The box was about half a cubit in size and served as a salt-cellar.
This was Cellini's first earning at Rome.
Cellini sent part of his earnings to his father and kept the rest for himself.
Cellini studied the antiquities of Rome while living on his earnings.
Battista del Tasso did not stay long in Rome and went back to Florence.
Cellini changed his master after finishing some commissions.
A Milanese called Pagolo Arsago worried Cellini into changing his master.
Il Firenzuola quarreled with Arsago about Cellini leaving.
Cellini defended his new master Arsago.
Cellini said he was born free and meant to live free.
Cellini claimed some crowns of wages were still due to him from Il Firenzuola.
Cellini said he chose to go where it pleased him as a free journeyman.
Arsago said he had not asked Cellini to come and suggested he return to Il Firenzuola.
Cellini said he had completed Il Firenzuola's commissions and chose to be his own master.
Il Firenzuola told Cellini not to show himself on his premises again.
Cellini reminded Il Firenzuola of the money owed to him.
Il Firenzuola laughed at Cellini's demand for payment.
Cellini said he could use his sword as well as his tools to claim just payment.
Maestro Antonio of San Marino came up during the dispute.
Maestro Antonio was the chief among the Roman goldsmiths.
Maestro Antonio had been Il Firenzuola's master.
Maestro Antonio espoused Cellini's cause and bade Il Firenzuola pay him.
Il Firenzuola was an admirable swordsman but not as good a goldsmith.
Cellini backed his cause with spirit until he got himself paid.
Cellini and Il Firenzuola became friends in course of time.
Cellini stood godfather to one of Il Firenzuola's children at his request.
Cellini worked with Pagolo Arsago and earned a good deal of money.
Cellini sent the greater part of his earnings to his father.
After two years, Cellini returned to Florence at his father's entreaty.
Cellini put himself under Francesco Salimbene again in Florence.
Cellini earned a great deal with Francesco Salimbene.
Cellini took continual pains to improve in his art.
Cellini renewed his intimacy with Francesco di Filippo.
Cellini was too much given to pleasure owing to music.
Cellini devoted some hours of the day or night to study despite his love of pleasure.
Cellini fashioned a silver heart's-key called a 'chiavaquore'.
The heart's-key was a girdle three inches broad made for brides.
The heart's-key was executed in half relief with small figures in the round.
Raffaello Lapaccini commissioned the heart's-key from Cellini.
Cellini was very badly paid for the heart's-key.
The honour brought by the heart's-key was worth more than the gain Cellini might have justly made.
Cellini had worked with many different persons in Florence.
Cellini knew worthy men among the goldsmiths including Marcone.
Some goldsmiths reputed honest did all they could to ruin Cellini and robbed him grossly.
Cellini left the company of dishonest goldsmiths and held them for thieves and black-guards.
Giovanbattista Sogliani was one of the goldsmiths Cellini encountered in Florence.
Cellini earned a great deal of money and took continual pains to improve in his art.
Cellini never neglected to devote some hours of the day or night to study.
Cellini fashioned a silver heart's-key called a chiavaquore.
The chiavaquore was a girdle three inches broad made for brides.
Cellini received a commission for the chiavaquore from a man called Raffaello Lapaccini.
Cellini was very badly paid for the chiavaquore.
The honour from the chiavaquore was worth more to Cellini than the financial gain.
Cellini worked with many different persons in Florence.
Cellini came to know worthy goldsmiths including Marcone, his first master.
Some goldsmiths reputed honest tried to ruin Cellini and robbed him grossly.
Cellini left the company of dishonest goldsmiths and held them for thieves and blackguards.
Giovanbattista Sogliani kindly accommodated Cellini with part of his shop.
Sogliani's shop stood at the side of the New Market near the Landi's bank.
Cellini finished several pretty pieces in Sogliani's shop and made good gains.
Cellini was able to give his family much help from his earnings.
Cellini's success roused the jealousy of bad men among his former masters.
The jealous masters were called Salvadore and Michele Guasconti.
Salvadore and Michele Guasconti had three big shops in the goldsmiths' guild.
Salvadore and Michele Guasconti drove a thriving trade.
Cellini complained to worthy fellows about the evil will of Salvadore and Michele Guasconti.
Cellini remarked that Salvadore and Michele Guasconti should have been satisfied with their thieveries.
Salvadore and Michele Guasconti threatened to make Cellini sorely repent of his words.
Cellini knew not what the colour of fear was.
Cellini paid little or no heed to the threats.
Gherardo Guasconti was the cousin of Salvadore and Michele Guasconti.
Gherardo Guasconti pushed a load of bricks violently onto Cellini's body.
Cellini struck Gherardo Guasconti a blow on the temple that made him fall down stunned.
Cellini told Salvadore and Michele Guasconti that was the way to treat cowardly thieves.
Cellini drew a little knife and threatened to kill anyone who came out of the shop.
Cellini's words frightened Salvadore and Michele Guasconti so that not one stirred to help their cousin.
The fathers and sons ran to the Eight and declared Cellini had assaulted them with sword in hand.
The magistrates had Cellini summoned.

Michel Agnolo reacted with great amusement when Cellini unveiled Diego.
Michel Agnolo knelt and called out for mercy upon seeing Diego.
Diego gave Michel Agnolo a papal benediction.
Diego blushed deeply when Michel Agnolo kissed his cheek.
The members of the club had written sonnets about Diego.
Diego read the sonnets aloud gracefully.
Giulio Romano compared the women to crows and Diego to a peacock.
Giulio Romano arranged the seating so that Diego sat in the center among the women.
Cellini was placed in the middle among the men.
Natural jasmines in full beauty were arranged as a background to the women.
Diego sang his part in the music better than almost all the rest.
Giulio Romano and Michel Agnolo dropped their banter and expressed sober heartfelt admiration for Diego.
Aurelio Ascolano was an improvisatory poet who extolled the women in choice phrases.
The two women sitting with Diego asked him personal questions about his life.
Diego was christened Pomona for the occasion.
Diego claimed to be a month or so with child when asked if he felt indisposed.
The women discovered Diego's true sex when they felt his body.
The women withdrew their hands and rose from the table with gibing words.
Michel Agnolo lifted Cellini aloft and called him a gentleman as punishment for the trick.
Michel Agnolo said the trick was deserving of punishment but also worthy of praise.
Cellini devoted himself with sustained diligence to acquiring mastery in several branches of art.
Michel Agnolo was making the monument of Pope Adrian at that time.
Giulio Romano went to paint for the Marquis of Mantua.
The other members of the club went in different directions to their own business.
Cellini devoted himself with sustained diligence and industry to acquiring mastery in several branches of art.
Cellini executed many and diverse pieces of work for a great variety of men.
Michel Agnolo, a Sienese sculptor, was making the monument of Pope Adrian at that time.
Turkish poniards with iron handles, blades, and sheaths engraved with foliage and filled with gold came into Cellini's hands.
The sight of Turkish poniards stirred in Cellini a great desire to try his skill in that branch of work.
Cellini found that he succeeded to his satisfaction in engraving poniards.
Cellini's engraved poniards were far more beautiful and more durable than Turkish ones.
Cellini cut his grooves much deeper and with wider trenches in steel than Turkish artisans did.
Turkish arabesques are composed only of arum leaves and a few small sunflowers.
Lombards construct beautiful patterns by copying the leaves of briony and ivy in exquisite curves.
Tuscans and Romans make a better choice by imitating the leaves of the acanthus with its stalks and flowers.
Italian arabesques can excellently well include figures of little birds and different animals.
Arabesques are called grotesques by the ignorant.
The term grotesque comes from arabesques being found in subterranean caverns in Rome.
The proper name for these patterns should be monsters, not grotesques.
Cellini designed patterns filled with gold that were far more pleasing to the eye than Turkish work.
Cellini found antique iron rings inlaid with gold set with tiny cameos of shell.
Ancient rings with cameos were worn as amulets by folk desirous of abiding with mind unshaken in extraordinary circumstances.
Cellini fabricated rings of refined steel engraved and inlaid with gold at the request of certain noblemen.
A single ring sometimes brought Cellini more than forty crowns in payment for his labour.
It was the custom in that epoch to wear little golden medals with devices engraved upon them in the cap.
Cellini made very many golden medals and found them extremely difficult to work.
Caradosso was an admirable craftsman who made such ornaments and asked at least a hundred gold crowns for his fee.
Cellini made a medal in competition with Caradosso that had four figures upon which he expended an infinity of labour.
Noblemen declared that Cellini's medal was by far better executed and more beautiful than Caradosso's.
Cellini replied that his greatest reward was to have rivalled the masterpieces of Caradosso.
Cellini's patrons sent him a very liberal present after he declined to name a price.
There grew in Cellini so great a spirit to do well after this event.
Pantasilea was a woman who felt false and fulsome love for Cellini.
Pantasilea was furiously enraged because of a pleasant trick by which Cellini brought Diego to a banquet.
Pantasilea swore to be revenged on Cellini.
Luigi Pulci was the son of one of the Pulcis who had been beheaded for incest with his daughter.
Luigi Pulci possessed extraordinary gifts for poetry together with sound Latin scholarship.
Luigi Pulci wrote well, was graceful in manners, and of surprising personal beauty.
Luigi Pulci had just left the service of some bishop and was thoroughly tainted with a very foul disease.
While a lad in Florence, Luigi Pulci ranked among the best improvisatori and sang on public streets during summer nights.
Michel Agnolo Buonarroti went with greatest eagerness and delight to listen to Luigi Pulci's recitations.
Piloto, a goldsmith, joined Buonarroti and Cellini on occasions to listen to Luigi Pulci.
Cellini and Luigi Pulci became acquainted through these occasions.
Luigi Pulci came to Rome in miserable plight and beseeched Cellini for help.
Cellini was moved to compassion by Luigi Pulci's great talents, love of fatherland, and his own natural tenderness of heart.
Cellini took Luigi Pulci into his house and had him medically treated.
Luigi Pulci soon regained his health being but a youth.
While pursuing his cure, Luigi Pulci never omitted his studies.
Cellini provided Luigi Pulci with books according to the means at his disposal.
Luigi Pulci returned thanks to Cellini with words and tears, protesting he would recompense him if God put good fortune in his way.
Cellini replied that he had not done for Luigi as much as he desired, but only what he could.
Cellini suggested that Luigi repay his service by doing likewise to someone who might have the same need.
Luigi Pulci began to frequent the Court of Rome and found a situation in the suite of a bishop of eighty years called Gurgensis.
The bishop Gurgensis had a nephew called Messer Giovanni, a nobleman of Venice.
Messer Giovanni made show of marvellous attachment to Luigi Pulci's talents.
Messer Giovanni brought Luigi Pulci as familiar to himself as his own flesh and blood.
Luigi Pulci talked to Messer Giovanni of Cellini and his great obligations to him.
Messer Giovanni expressed a wish to make Cellini's acquaintance.
Cellini invited Pantasilea to supper and assembled a company of men of parts who were his friends.
Messer Giovanni and Luigi Pulci arrived at Cellini's supper just as the company was sitting down to table.
Pantasilea cast her eyes upon Luigi Pulci's beauty and began to lay her nets for him.
Cellini took Luigi Pulci aside after supper and conjured him to have nothing whatever to do with Pantasilea.
Luigi Pulci answered that Cellini should not take him for a madman.
Cellini swore to Luigi Pulci by God that he did not give Pantasilea the least thought but was sorry for Luigi's sake.
Luigi Pulci vowed and prayed to God that if ever he but spoke with Pantasilea, he might upon the moment break his neck.
Messer Giovanni showed signs too evident of loving Luigi Pulci in a dishonourable way.
Luigi Pulci had new suits of silk and velvet every morning.
Luigi Pulci abandoned himself altogether to bad courses.
Luigi Pulci neglected his fine talents and pretended not to see or recognise Cellini.
Cellini had once rebuked Luigi Pulci and told him he was giving his soul to foul vices.
Messer Giovanni bought Luigi Pulci a very fine black horse for which he paid 150 crowns.
The black horse was admirably trained to hand so that Luigi could go daily to caracole around Pantasilea's lodgings.
Cellini took notice of Luigi's horse but paid it no attention, remarking that all things acted as their nature prompted.
Cellini gave his whole mind to his studies while Luigi pursued Pantasilea.
On a Sunday evening in summer, Cellini was invited to sup with Michel Agnolo, the Sienese sculptor.
Bachiacca was present at the party and had brought with him his old flame, Pantasilea.
Pantasilea sat between Cellini and Bachiacca at the table.
Pantasilea rose in the middle of the banquet and excused herself upon the pretext of a natural need.
Pantasilea remained an unaccountably long time absent from the supper.
Cellini heard a sort of subdued tittering in the street below.
Cellini had a knife in hand which he was using for his service at the table.
Cellini rose and saw Luigi in the street together with Pantasilea.
Cellini heard Luigi saying that if Benvenuto saw them, they would catch it.
Cellini leaped from the window and took Luigi by the cape.
Cellini would have killed Luigi with the knife he held, but Luigi was riding a white horse.
Luigi clapped spurs to his horse and left his cape in Cellini's grasp to preserve his life.
Pantasilea took to her heels in the direction of a neighbouring church.
The company at supper rose immediately and came down entreating Cellini to refrain from inconvenience for a strumpet.
Cellini told them he was bent on punishing the infamous young man, not moved on Pantasilea's account.
Cellini took his sword and went alone toward Prati.
The house where Cellini was supping stood close to the Castello gate, which led to Prati.
Cellini walked upon the road to Prati and had not gone far before the sun sank.
Cellini re-entered Rome itself at a slow pace.
Night had fallen and darkness had come on, but the gates of Rome were not yet shut.
Toward two hours after sunset, Cellini walked along Pantasilea's lodging with the intention of doing something to the discontent of both if Luigi were there.
Cellini heard and saw that only a poor servant-girl called Canida was in Pantasilea's house.
Cellini went to put away his cloak and the scabbard of his sword.
Pantasilea's house stood behind the Banchi on the river Tiber.
Just opposite Pantasilea's house stretched a garden belonging to an innkeeper called Romolo.
The garden was enclosed by a thick hedge of thorns.
Cellini hid himself in the hedge, standing upright, and waiting till the woman came back with Luigi.
Cellini's friend Bachiacca crept up to him while he was keeping watch in the hedge.
Bachiacca called out to Cellini using the name 'Gossip' which they used for fun.
Bachiacca prayed Cellini for God's love not to injure Pantasilea, saying she had erred in no wise.
Cellini replied that if Bachiacca did not take himself off, he would bring his sword down upon his head.
Bachiacca was overwhelmed with fright and suddenly taken ill with the colic.
Bachiacca withdrew to ease himself apart.
There was a glorious heaven of stars which shed good light to see by.
All of a sudden Cellini was aware of the noise of many horses coming toward him from one side and the other.
It turned out to be Luigi and Pantasilea, attended by Messer Benvegnato of Perugia, who was chamberlain to Pope Clement.
Messer Benvegnato was followed by four doughty captains of Perugia with some other valiant soldiers in the flower of youth.
Altogether there were more than twelve swords in the group.
Cellini did his best to crouch into the hedge when he understood the matter and saw not how to fly.
Cellini threatened to strike someone with his sword if they did not leave.
Cellini was hiding in a hedge when Luigi and Pantasilea arrived with armed men.
Cellini struck Luigi on the shoulder with his sword during the confrontation.
Cellini's sword blow hit Pantasilea in the nose and mouth.
Cellini engaged in combat with multiple armed opponents and escaped with honor.
Cellini was taken in by a great Neapolitan nobleman who appreciated his fighting abilities.
The Neapolitan nobleman helped arrange a peace reconciliation between Cellini and his opponents.
Luigi Pulci died from a broken leg sustained when he fell from his horse.
Cellini believed God keeps account of the good and bad and gives each person what they merit.
Cellini took much pleasure in music and amusements.
Cellini was appointed captain of fifty young men to protect Alessandro del Bene's palace.
Cellini participated in reconnaissance of the walls during Bourbon's siege of Rome.
Cellini fired an arquebuse at the Constable of Bourbon and believes he killed him.
Cellini entered the Castle of Sant'Angelo during the sack of Rome.
Cellini took command of artillery at the castle when other bombardiers were emotionally unable to fire.
Cellini was stationed with five pieces of artillery on the highest point of the castle.
Cellini believed he was more naturally inclined to the profession of arms than to his adopted art.
Cellini took greater pleasure in his duties as a gunner than in his work as an artist.
Cellini was paid in advance and given rations of bread and wine for his artillery service.
A cannon shot struck Cellini in the chest during his artillery practice at the castle.
Cellini practiced artillery continuously during the month-long siege of the castle.
Cellini was ordered by Messer Antonio Santacroce to fire on houses where besiegers had been seen.
A cannon shot hit the battlement near Cellini and struck him in the chest, knocking him unconscious.
Messer Antonio Santacroce lamented that Cellini was the best defender they had.
Gianfrancesco, a bandsman and naturally gifted in medicine, treated Cellini's injury with wormwood and Greek wine.
Soldiers filled Cellini's mouth with earth, believing they were giving him the sacrament.
Cellini returned to his artillery work after recovering from the cannon blast.
Pope Clement sent a demand for assistance to the Duke of Urbino.
Cellini was charged with lighting beacons and firing guns as a signal that the castle had not yielded.
Cellini pointed his artillery by day at places where he could do the most damage.
The Pope began to regard Cellini with greater favor because of his intelligent discharge of his duties.
Aid from the Duke of Urbino never came.
Cardinals of Ravenna and de' Gaddi frequently came to watch Cellini work.
Cellini told the cardinals not to show themselves because their red caps made them targets.
Cellini had the cardinals locked off from the artillery area, which caused them to regard him with ill-will.
General Orazio Baglioni was well affected toward Cellini.
Orazio Baglioni ordered Cellini to fire at a tavern called Baccanello where he believed important soldiers were gathered.
Cellini hit the sun painted on the tavern wall exactly in its center.
A barrel of stones fell from the cannon's blast and nearly struck Cardinal Farnese and Messer Jacopo Salviati.
Cellini prepared to defend himself with two loaded falconets when he heard people say it would be good to kill him.
Cellini threatened to fire on anyone who attempted to come up the staircase.
Orazio Baglioni came to Cellini and expressed great love for him.
Orazio Baglioni asked Cellini not to say he was present when the gun was fired.
Cellini had previously wanted to avenge his father against Messer Jacopo Salviati.
Cellini killed one or more enemies of the besieging army nearly every day.
The Pope observed a Spanish Colonel in the Prati whom he recognized as a former servant.
Cellini fired a gerfalcon at a man dressed in rose-color and hit him in the middle, cutting him in two.
The Pope asked Cellini to explain how he achieved such a distant shot.
Cellini knelt and asked the Pope for pardon for the homicide and all murders committed in service of the Church.
The Pope blessed Cellini and gave him pardon for all murders he had committed or would commit in service of the Apostolic Church.
Cellini's drawing, studies, and music were consumed by the work of artillery.
The enemy loaded the roof of a house with thirty gabions to obstruct Cellini's view.
Cellini directed his five pieces of artillery at the gabions and fired when the enemy changed guard.
Cellini's blast destroyed the gabions and killed more than thirty men.
Cellini repeated this maneuver twice, causing disorder among the enemy soldiers.
The enemy soldiers were forced to take a different route that cost them three miles of march instead of half a mile.
Cellini was entreated with prodigious favors by men of quality in the castle after this feat.
Pope Clement called Cellini to help save the tiaras and jewels of the Apostolic Camera.
The Pope shut himself up with Cellini and the Cavalierino to handle the papal jewels.
The Cavalierino was French, of the lowest birth, but had been made rich by the Pope.
Cellini removed all gems from their gold settings.
Cellini wrapped the gems in paper and they were sewn into the linings of the Pope's and Cavaliere's clothes.
The gold weighed about two hundred pounds.
Cellini was ordered to melt down the gold secretly.
Cellini built a small draught-furnace of bricks in his lodging at the Angel.
Cellini remained at Florence on the entreaty of his brother and sister, though his own inclination led him to return to Rome.
Piero, son of Giovanni Landi, was a dear friend who had helped Cellini in earlier troubles.
The Medici were in exile at that time, specifically Signor Ippolito and Signor Alessandro.
A Sienese named Girolamo Marretti, who had lived long in Turkey, came to Cellini's shop and commissioned him to make a golden medal.
The subject of the medal was Hercules wrenching the lion's mouth.
Michelangelo Buonarroti came often to see Cellini's medal while he was working on it.
Cellini spent infinite pains upon the design of the medal.
Michelangelo praised Cellini's work on the medal.
Cellini found little else to do but jewel-setting.
Cellini was dissatisfied with jewel-setting and wished to be employed upon some higher task.
Federigo Ginori was a young man of lofty spirit who had lived some years in Naples.
Federigo Ginori had been the lover of a Neapolitan princess.
Federigo Ginori wanted to have a medal made with Atlas bearing the world upon his shoulders.
Federigo Ginori applied to Michelangelo for a design of the Atlas medal.
Michelangelo recommended Cellini to Federigo Ginori as a young goldsmith who would serve him admirably.
Michelangelo offered to sketch a design for the Atlas medal while suggesting Cellini also make a model.
Cellini set himself to work with eagerness upon the wax model for the Atlas medal.
Giuliano Bugiardini, a painter intimate with Michelangelo, brought Cellini the drawing of Atlas.
Cellini's wax model for the Atlas medal was very different from Michelangelo's drawing.
Federigo Ginori and Bugiardini agreed that Cellini should work upon his own model.
The Atlas medal was a figure chiselled on a plate of gold.
The Atlas medal featured a crystal ball engraved with the zodiac upon a field of lapis-lazuli.
The legend on the Atlas medal read 'Summa tulisse juvat!'
Federigo Ginori paid Cellini very liberally for the Atlas medal.
Aluigi Alamanni was in Florence at that time.
Federigo Ginori brought Aluigi Alamanni often to Cellini's workshop.
Cellini and Aluigi Alamanni became very intimate together through this introduction.
Pope Clement declared war upon the city of Florence.
Florence was put in a state of defence.
Militia was organised in each quarter of Florence.
Cellini received orders to serve in the militia in his turn.
Cellini provided himself with a rich outfit for militia service.
Cellini went about with the highest nobility of Florence.
The highest nobility of Florence showed a unanimous desire to fight for the defence of their liberties.
A letter came to Cellini from a man called Maestro Giacopino della Barca.
Maestro Giacopino della Barca's real name was Giacopo della Sciorina.
Maestro Giacopino della Barca kept a ferry boat upon the Tiber between Ponte Sisto and Ponte Santo Agnolo.
Maestro Giacopino della Barca was a person of considerable talent distinguished by his pleasantries and striking conversation.
Maestro Giacopino della Barca had formerly been a designer of patterns for cloth-weavers in Florence.
Maestro Giacopino della Barca was intimate with the Pope.
The Pope took great pleasure in hearing Maestro Giacopino della Barca talk.
The Pope spoke of Cellini in the very highest terms.
The Pope said he would be glad to get Cellini back if he knew where he was.
Maestro Giacopino della Barca told the Pope that Cellini was in Florence.
The Pope bade Maestro Giacopino della Barca write and tell Cellini to return to him.
Cellini wrote to Maestro Giacopino della Barca begging him not to write to him again.
Maestro Giacopino della Barca wrote Cellini another letter despite his request.
The second letter from Maestro Giacopino della Barca was extravagantly worded.
The letter stated that the Pope required Cellini to come at once.
The letter said the Pope wanted to employ Cellini on work of the greatest consequence.
The letter urged Cellini to throw up everything and not stand against the Pope in the party of Radicals.
Cellini was put in great fright by the second letter from Maestro Giacopino della Barca.
Cellini went to seek his dear friend Piero Landi.
Cellini asked Piero Landi to return the gems and gold in his drawers to certain persons.
Cellini asked Piero Landi to pack up the furniture of his house.
Piero Landi replied that Cellini should go quickly and write to him.
Piero Landi was the most loyal friend Cellini had ever known.
Piero Landi was the wisest, most worthy, most discreet, and most affectionate friend Cellini had ever known.
Cellini left Florence and went to Rome.
Cellini wrote to Piero Landi from Rome.
Upon his arrival in Rome, Cellini found several of his former friends.
Cellini's former friends received him very well and entertained him kindly.
Cellini set himself to work at things which brought him profit but were not notable enough to be described.
There was a fine old man goldsmith called Raffaello del Moro who had considerable reputation in the trade.
Raffaello del Moro was a very worthy fellow.
Raffaello del Moro begged Cellini to enter his workshop.
Raffaello del Moro said he had some commissions of importance to execute with high profits.
Cellini accepted Raffaello del Moro's proposal with goodwill.
More than ten days elapsed before Cellini presented himself to Maestro Giacopino della Barca.
Maestro Giacopino della Barca met Cellini by accident and gave him a hearty welcome.
Maestro Giacopino della Barca asked Cellini how long he had been in Rome.
Cellini told Maestro Giacopino della Barca he had been in Rome about a fortnight.
Maestro Giacopino della Barca took it ill that Cellini had not presented himself sooner.
Maestro Giacopino della Barca said Cellini showed little esteem for the Pope.
Maestro Giacopino della Barca said the Pope had urgently compelled him to write three times for Cellini.
Cellini had taken Maestro Giacopino della Barca's persistence in the matter ill.
Cellini made no reply to Maestro Giacopino della Barca's complaint but swallowed down his irritation.
Maestro Giacopino della Barca suffered from a flux of words.
Cellini said Maestro Giacopino della Barca might bring him to the Pope when he saw fit.
Maestro Giacopino della Barca answered that any time would do for him.
Cellini said he was always ready.
Cellini and Maestro Giacopino della Barca took their way toward the Pope's palace.
It was a Maundy Thursday when Cellini went to see the Pope.
The Pope was in bed, suffering from a slight indisposition.
Messer Jacopo Salviati and the Archbishop of Capua were with the Pope.
The Pope was exceedingly glad when he set eyes on Cellini.
Cellini kissed the Pope's feet.
Cellini drew near to the Pope as humbly as he could.
Cellini let the Pope understand that he had things of consequence to utter.
The Pope waved his hand and the two prelates retired to a distance.
Cellini told the Pope that from the time of the sack up to that hour, he had never been able to confess or communicate.
Cellini said they refused him absolution.
Cellini said that when he melted down the gold and worked at unsetting jewels, the Pope ordered the Cavalierino to give him a modest reward.
Cellini said he received nothing from the Cavalierino but abuse.
Cellini said he found about a pound and a half of gold in tiny grains like millet-seeds when he washed the ashes.
Cellini said he did not have money enough to take him home respectably.
Cellini said he thought he would avail himself of the gold and give it back when opportunity should offer.
Cellini asked the Pope to grant him indulgence so he could confess and communicate.
The Pope said he thoroughly believed what Cellini told him.
The Pope said it was in his power to absolve Cellini of any unbecoming deed.
The Pope said he had the will to absolve Cellini.
The Pope said if Cellini had taken the value of a whole tiara, he was quite ready to pardon him.
Cellini said he took nothing but what he had confessed.
Cellini said what he took did not amount to the value of 140 ducats.
Cellini said he received 140 ducats from the Mint in Perugia.
Cellini said he went home to comfort his poor old father with the money.
The Pope said Cellini's father had been as virtuous, good, and worthy a man as was ever born.
The Pope said Cellini had not degenerated from his father.
The Pope said he was very sorry the money was so little.
The Pope made Cellini a present of the money and gave him full pardon.
The Pope told Cellini to assure his confessor of the pardon.
The Pope said that after Cellini had confessed and communicated, he should present himself again.
The Pope said it would be to Cellini's advantage to present himself again.
When Cellini parted from the Pope, Messer Giacopo and the Archbishop approached.
The Pope spoke to Messer Giacopo and the Archbishop in the highest terms imaginable about Cellini.
The Pope said he had confessed and absolved Cellini.
The Pope commissioned the Archbishop of Capua to send for Cellini and ask if he had any other need.
The Pope gave the Archbishop of Capua full leave to absolve Cellini amply.
The Pope bade the Archbishop of Capua treat Cellini with the utmost kindness.
While walking away with Maestro Giacopino, the latter asked Cellini inquisitively what was the close and lengthy conversation with the Pope.
Maestro Giacopino repeated the question more than twice.
Cellini said he did not mean to tell Maestro Giacopino because they were matters with which he had nothing to do.
Cellini went to do what had been agreed on with the Pope.
After two festivals were over, Cellini again presented himself before the Pope.
The Pope received Cellini even better than before.
The Pope said if Cellini had come a little earlier to Rome, he would have commissioned him to restore his two tiaras.
The Pope said his two tiaras were pulled to pieces in the castle.
The Pope said the tiaras, with the exception of the gems, were objects of little artistic interest.
The Pope said he would employ Cellini on a piece of the very greatest consequence.
The Pope said the piece would allow Cellini to exhibit all his talents.
The Pope said the piece was a button for his priest's cope.
The Pope said the button had to be made round like a trencher.
The Pope said the button had to be as big as a little trencher, one-third of a cubit wide.
The Pope wanted Cellini to represent a God the Father in half-relief on the button.
The Pope wanted Cellini to set a magnificent big diamond in the middle of the button.
The Pope wanted Cellini to set several other gems of the greatest value on the button.
The Pope said Caradosso began to make a button but did not finish it.
The Pope wanted Cellini's button to be finished quickly so he could enjoy the use of it.
The Pope told Cellini to make a fine model.
The Pope had all the jewels shown to Cellini.
Cellini went off like a shot to set himself to work on the button.
During the time when Florence was besieged, Federigo Ginori died of consumption.
The Atlas medal came into the hands of Messer Luigi Alamanni after Federigo Ginori's death.
Messer Luigi Alamanni took the Atlas medal to present in person to Francis, king of France.
Messer Luigi Alamanni accompanied the medal with some of his own finest compositions.
King Francis was exceedingly delighted with the gift of the Atlas medal.
Messer Luigi Alamanni told King Francis much about Cellini's personal qualities and art.
Messer Luigi Alamanni spoke so favourably about Cellini that the King expressed a wish to know him.
Cellini's family had Medicean sympathies.
Cellini's father served Lorenzo and Piero de' Medici.
Cellini's brother was a soldier of Giovanni delle Bande Nere and Duke Alessandro.
Cellini made a medal of Atlas for Federigo Ginori.
Federigo Ginori died of consumption during the siege of Florence.
The medal of Atlas came into the hands of Messer Luigi Alamanni.
Messer Luigi Alamanni presented the medal to Francis, king of France.
The King of France expressed a wish to know Cellini.
Cellini constructed a model for a button at the exact size the jewel was meant to have.
Cellini's button model roused considerable jealousy among goldsmiths.
Micheletto was very clever at engraving cornelians.
Micheletto was an old man of great celebrity.
Micheletto had been employed upon the Pope's tiaras.
Michele ordered more than thirty drawings to be made for the Pope's commissioned piece.
Pompeo was a Milanese jeweller in favour with the Pope.
Pompeo was related to Messer Traiano, the first chamberlain of the court.
The draughtsmen employed by Micheletto were not in the jeweller's trade.
All of Micheletto's designs placed the famous diamond in the middle of the breast of God the Father.
The Pope approved of none of Micheletto's designs.
Cellini's model impressed the Pope greatly.
Cellini promised to work the button in gold ten times better than the model.
An eminent philosopher predicted that Cellini would accomplish what he said.
The Pope gave Cellini five hundred golden ducats of the Camera.
Cellini placed the diamond exactly in the center of his design.
Cellini depicted God the Father seated and leaning in a sideways attitude above the diamond.
Cellini placed three children below the diamond supporting the jewel.
Cellini's work was executed in white stucco on a black stone.
Cellini laboured diligently for eight days on the golden plate.
The Pope sent for Cellini after eight days of work.
The Pope intended to entrust Cellini with furnishing dies for the money of the Mint.
Cellini had seen how dies were made but had never made any.
Messer Tommaso of Prato was the Pope's Datary.
Messer Tommaso opposed the Pope's commissions to Cellini.
The Pope commanded Cellini to make a model for a broad doubloon of gold.
The doubloon was to depict a naked Christ with his hands tied and the inscription 'Ecce Homo.'
The reverse of the doubloon was to show a Pope and Emperor propping up a cross.
Bandinello the sculptor suggested that goldsmiths needed drawings for fine work.
Cellini responded that he did not want Bandinello's drawings.
Cellini made two dies of steel very quickly.
Cellini stamped a coin in gold and brought it to the Pope on a Sunday after dinner.
Cellini brought old coins made for Popes Giulio and Leo to compare with his work.
Cellini's coin pleased the Pope far better than the old coins.
Cellini submitted a petition for the post of stamp-master in the Mint.
The post of stamp-master was worth six golden crowns a month.
Dies were paid at the rate of a ducat for three by the Master of the Mint.
The Pope signed Cellini's patent with his own hand.
Cellini worked in the shop of Raffaello del Moro.
Raffaello del Moro had a very beautiful young daughter.
Raffaello del Moro had designs on Cellini regarding his daughter.
Cellini was willing to reciprocate Raffaello's intentions.
