The Pope became ill and his physicians thought the case was dangerous.
Cellini's enemy engaged Neapolitan soldiers to kill him.
Cellini completed the reverse and brought it to the Pope, who was in bed in a deplorable condition.
The Pope died three days after receiving the medals from Cellini.
Cellini kissed the feet of the dead Pope and shed tears.
Pompeo went by with ten well-armed men and stopped as though to pick a quarrel with Cellini.
Cellini's companions wanted him to draw his sword, but he refused to do so.
Cellini told his companions he was able to conduct his quarrels by himself.
Albertaccio del Bene was Cellini's dearest comrade and loved him like himself.
Cellini asked Albertaccio not to attend to him in the matter with Pompeo.
Pompeo had boasted of the insult he fancied he had put upon Cellini.
Cellini stabbed Pompeo beneath the ear with a dagger at the corner of Chiavica.
Cellini gave Pompeo two blows, and Pompeo fell stone dead at the second.
Cellini had not meant to kill Pompeo.
Cellini walked about three hundred paces after the stabbing before meeting Piloto the goldsmith.
Piloto advised Cellini to go to Albertaccio del Bene's house.
The young men of the Banchi came to Albertaccio's house to help save Cellini's life.
Messer Luigi Rucellai and other great folk offered their services to Cellini.
The young men blessed Cellini's hands, approving of his killing of Pompeo.
Cardinal Cornaro despatched thirty soldiers to bring Cellini to his quarters.
Messer Traiano sent a Milanese to Cardinal de' Medici to report Cellini's crime.
Cardinal de' Medici said Cellini's crime would have been great if he had not committed the lesser one.
Cardinal de' Medici told the Bishop of Frulli to search after Cellini and help defend him.
The Bishop of Frulli visited Cellini at Cardinal Cornaro's palace.
Cardinal Cornaro flew into a rage when he heard Cardinal de' Medici wanted to protect Cellini.
Cellini went to pay his respects to Cardinal de' Medici without Cornaro's knowledge.
Cardinal Farnese was elected Pope a few days after Cellini's visit to Cardinal de' Medici.
The new Pope sent for Cellini, saying he did not wish anyone else to strike his coins.
Messer Latino Juvinale informed the Pope that Cellini was in hiding for a murder.
The Pope replied that men like Cellini stand above the law.
The Pope granted Cellini a safe-conduct.
Cellini began to serve the new Pope and was treated with the utmost favor.
Cellini designed a S. Paul for the coins with the inscription 'Vas electionis.'
The Pope forbade anyone else to speak to him of coins, wishing only Cellini to have to do with them.
The Pope told Cellini he must first obtain pardon for the homicide at the holy Maries' day in August.
The Pope promised Cellini another safe-conduct to keep him in security until August.
Pompeo had left three thousand ducats as dowry to an illegitimate daughter.
Signor Pier Luigi arranged for a favorite of his to marry Pompeo's daughter.
Signor Pier Luigi laid his hands on the dowry money.
Signor Pier Luigi promised to have Cellini taken up when the first flush of his favor with the Pope had passed.
Signor Pier Luigi commissioned a Corsican soldier to assassinate Cellini or have him taken up by the Bargello.
Cellini's enemies promised the Corsican soldier a reward of one hundred crowns.
Cellini went about with good attendance, wearing an under-coat and armlets of mail.
The Corsican soldier was sent to intercept Cellini on Strada Giulia.
Cellini observed the Corsican get up and take his station in the middle of the road.
Cellini spoke to the Corsican soldier, telling him the job was more dangerous than he imagined.
Cellini told the Corsican he would defend himself against a hundred men.
Cellini perceived from a soldier's gestures that the soldier intended to do him mischief.
Cellini claimed he had never had anything to do with the soldier and never injured him.
Cellini stated he would defend himself against a hundred men.
A Corsican informed Cellini through a mutual friend that he wished to be Cellini's good brother.
The Corsican warned Cellini that he was in greatest peril from people of much consequence who had sworn to have his life.
Signor Pier Luigi gave orders for Cellini to be taken that evening.
Cellini left for Florence at twenty-three o'clock (11 PM) after receiving warning of the arrest order.
Duke Alessandro greeted Cellini with extraordinary kindness and pressed him to remain in his service.
Cellini had stood godfather to Tribolo's son.
Tribolo had never seen Venice before and expected to gain money by going there.
Duke Alessandro exacted a formal promise from Cellini that he would return to serve him after visiting Venice.
Duke Alessandro bestowed fifty golden crowns on Cellini as a sign of good-will.
Cellini refused to bind his sword before leaving Florence, saying a man on horseback should not do so.
Tribolo was the most timorous man Cellini had ever known.
Cellini and Tribolo avoided speaking with Florentine exiles in Ferrara to prevent incurring banishment.
Cellini drew his sword against Niccolò Benintendi and the exiles at the inn in Ferrara.
Cellini brandished his sword along the walls while shouting threats but took care not to harm anyone.
The innkeeper told Benintendi that drawing swords in the inn was punishable by death under the Duke's law.
The innkeeper told Cellini he was entirely in the right and warned him to be on guard against those men on his journey.
Cellini asked a barge-man to let him and Tribolo have the boat to themselves.
Cellini bought a stout pike in Ferrara.
Tribolo was frightened when he saw Benintendi's brother and three comrades waiting at the port.
Cellini dismounted and marched boldly forward with his pike toward the waiting men.
Cellini leapt into the boat and turned his pike's point against the men.
Cellini struck Magalotti with his pike but chose not to kill him when he had the power to do so.
Cellini told Magalotti to take his arms and go away, saying he had shown he could not do what he did not want to do.
Cellini and his party traveled ten miles on the Po before the young men caught up in a skiff.
Piero Benintendi told Cellini they would meet in Venice.
Cellini applied to a brother of Cardinal Cornaro in Venice for permission to carry arms.
Cellini visited Jacopo del Sansovino, the sculptor, who received him most kindly and invited him to dinner.
Sansovino told Tribolo he had no work to give him at the moment.
Cellini laughed at Sansovino's response to Tribolo.
Cellini took Tribolo's part against Sansovino because Tribolo was in the right.
Sansovino spent dinner chattering about his great achievements and abusing Michel Agnolo and other sculptors.
Cellini found Sansovino's boasting so annoying that the food did not taste well to him.
Cellini told Sansovino that men of worth and genius shine forth better when others praise them than when they boast of their own achievements.
Cellini and Sansovino rose from table in anger after this exchange.
Cellini turned into an apothecary's shop in Venice to avoid a quarrel with Piero Benintendi and his companions.
Young Magalotti scolded Benintendi and his companions for trying to quarrel with Cellini.
Cellini took the part of his comrade Tribolo at a dinner because Tribolo was in the right.
Sansovino chatted copiously about his great achievements at dinner.
Sansovino abused Michelangelo and other fellow-sculptors.
Sansovino bragged and vaunted himself to the skies.
Cellini believed that men of worth and genius shine forth better when others praise them than when they boast of their own achievements.
Cellini and Sansovino rose from table in anger.
Cellini met Piero Benintendi near the Rialto who appeared to be going to pick a quarrel with him.
Cellini took refuge in an apothecary's shop to avoid the quarrel.
Young Magalotti scolded Piero Benintendi and his companions on Cellini's behalf.
Cellini and Tribolo set out to return to Florence.
They stayed one night at a place on this side Chioggia.
The host insisted on being paid before bed and in his own way.
Cellini argued that it was customary to pay in the morning elsewhere.
Tribolo was trembling with fear and nudged Cellini to keep quiet.
They paid the host in the way he wanted.
The beds at the inn were new, clean, and capital.
Cellini did not sleep because he was thinking about revenge.
Cellini considered setting fire to the house or cutting the throats of the host's horses.
Cellini resolved to put his and Tribolo's things on board the boat.
Cellini returned to the inn claiming he had left slippers in his bedroom.
The host told Cellini they could go to Jericho.
A ragged stable-boy told Cellini the master was in bed with a woman.
Cellini gave the stable-boy a few Venetian coppers.
Cellini cut four beds into ribbons with a sharp knife.
Cellini estimated the damage he did was more than fifty crowns.
Cellini ran to the boat with pieces of bed-covers in his pouch.
Tribolo wanted to go back for some little straps belonging to his carpet-bag.
Cellini told Tribolo he could make him as many big straps as he liked.
Cellini informed Tribolo of the trick he had played on the host.
Tribolo was thrown into quaking fright when he learned what Cellini had done.
Tribolo did not feel safe until they reached the gates of Florence.
Tribolo asked Cellini to bind their swords up and play no more games.
Cellini told Tribolo his sword had never been loosened.
Cellini observed that Tribolo's sword was tied just as he had arranged it before leaving his house.
Cellini believed Tribolo had acted worse by never coming to his assistance during the journey.
Cellini went to visit Duke Alessandro upon arriving in Florence.
Cellini thanked the Duke for a present of fifty crowns.
Cellini told the Duke he was ready to serve him according to his abilities.
The Duke ordered Cellini to strike dies for his coinage.
Cellini made a piece of forty soldi with the Duke's head on one side and San Cosimo and San Damiano on the other.
The Duke said Cellini's coins were the best pieces of money in Christendom.
All Florence agreed that Cellini's coins were the best.
Cellini asked the Duke for appointments and lodgings of the Mint.
The Duke promised Cellini more than he demanded.
The Duke commissioned Carlo Acciaiuoli as Master of the Mint.
Cellini drew money so discreetly that he always had something to his credit.
Cellini made dies for a giulio with San Giovanni in profile seated with a book.
Cellini believed the giulio was finer than anything he had done before.
Cellini made dies for half-giulios with the full face of San Giovanni in small.
Cellini's half-giulio was the first coin with a head in full face on such thin silver.
Cellini made dies for golden crowns with a cross and cherubim on one side and the Duke's arms on the other.
The Duke told Cellini he was quite satisfied with his service.
The Duke promised to grant Cellini his request for appointments and lodgings.
The Duke was looking at a remarkable little gun sent from Germany.
The Duke knew Cellini took pleasure in such things.
The Duke offered Cellini an arquebuse of his own liking from the armoury.
Cellini accepted the Duke's invitation and chose the finest arquebuse he ever saw.
The Duke ordered Pretino of Lucca to let Cellini take whatever he liked.
The Duke commissioned Cellini to make drawings for gold-work for his wife.
The Duke asked Cellini to execute a die of his portrait in fine style.
Cellini began the portrait in wax.
The Duke gave orders that Cellini should be admitted whenever he came to take his portrait.
Cellini sent for Pietro Pagolo from Monte Ritondo to help him.
Cellini found Pietro Pagolo with Bernardonaccio, a goldsmith who did not treat him well.
Cellini brought Pietro Pagolo away and taught him how to strike coins from the dies.
Cellini often found the Duke napping after dinner with Lorenzino.
Cellini marveled that the Duke showed such confidence about his safety.
Ottaviano de' Medici appeared to have the government of everything in his own hands.
Ottaviano de' Medici favoured the old Master of the Mint against the Duke's will.
The old Master of the Mint was called Bastiano Cennini.
Bastiano Cennini was an artist of the antiquated school with little skill.
Ottaviano mixed Cennini's stupid dies with Cellini's in the coinage of crown-pieces.
Cellini complained to the Duke about the mixed dies.
The Duke took the matter very ill.
The Duke told Cellini to show Ottaviano how the matter stood.
Ottaviano said they chose to have the dies mixed.
Cellini replied that it ought not to be so and that he did not choose it.
Cellini told Ottaviano the thing was neither just nor reasonable.
Ottaviano told Cellini to take himself off.
Cellini returned to the Duke and entreated him not to allow his fine coins to be spoiled.
Cellini begged the Duke for permission to leave Florence.
The Duke said Ottaviano was too presuming.
The Duke promised Cellini he should have what he wanted.
The Duke called the mixed dies an injury offered to himself.
Cellini received a full safe-conduct from the Pope on a Thursday.
The Pope advised Cellini to go to Rome and get the pardon of Our Lady's feast in mid-August.
The pardon would clear Cellini from penalties attaching to his homicide.
Cellini went to the Duke, whom he found in bed suffering from a debauch.
Cellini finished the waxen medal in little more than two hours.
The Duke was extremely pleased with the medal.
Cellini exhibited the safe-conduct sent by the Pope.
Cellini told the Duke that the Pope had recalled him to execute certain pieces of work.
Cellini wanted to regain his footing in Rome.
The Duke made answer half in anger.
The Duke asked Cellini to stay and promised to provide for his appointments.
The Duke promised Cellini lodgings in the Mint with much more than he could ask for.
The Duke asked who would be able to strike the beautiful dies Cellini had made.
Cellini said he had a pupil, a young Roman, whom he had taught the art.
Cellini said his pupil would serve the Duke well until he returned with the medal finished.
Cellini said he had a shop open in Rome with journeymen and a pretty business.
Cellini said he would leave the devotion of Rome to a pupil and return to the Duke.
Lorenzino de' Medici was present during the conversation with the Duke.
The Duke frequently signed to Lorenzino to join in pressing Cellini to stay.
Lorenzino said Cellini would do better to remain where he was.
Cellini answered that he wanted by all means to regain his hold on Rome.
Lorenzino continued eyeing the Duke with very evil glances.
Cellini said he would make the Duke a much finer medal than the one he made for Pope Clement.
Cellini said the first medal was the first he ever made.
Cellini asked Lorenzino to give him an exquisite reverse.
Cellini said Lorenzino was a person learned and of the greatest genius.
Lorenzino said he had been thinking of nothing else but how to give a reverse worthy of the Duke.
The Duke laughed and said Lorenzino should give the reverse and Cellini should do it in Florence.
Lorenzino said he would do it as quickly as he could.
Lorenzino said he hoped to do something that would make the whole world wonder.
The Duke held Lorenzino sometimes for a fool and sometimes for a coward.
The Duke laughed at Lorenzino's bragging words.
Cellini took his leave without further ceremony.
The Duke did not believe Cellini was really going.
The Duke sent a servant who caught up with Cellini at Siena.
The servant gave Cellini fifty golden ducats from the Duke.
The Duke's message said Cellini should return as soon as possible.
Lorenzino was preparing an admirable reverse for the medal.
Cellini left full directions to Pietro Pagolo on how to use the dies.
Pietro Pagolo never quite succeeded in employing the dies because it was a very delicate affair.
Cellini remained creditor to the Mint in a matter of more than seventy crowns on account of dies supplied by him.
Cellini carried a handsome arquebuse that the Duke gave him on his journey to Rome.
Cellini used the arquebuse several times during his journey and performed incredible feats with it.
Cellini's little house in Strada Giulia was not ready when he arrived in Rome.
Cellini had committed many of his arms and other things to the keeping of Messer Giovanni Gaddi before leaving Rome.
Cellini had a partner named Felice.
Cellini had two young serving-lads.
A laundress lived beneath Cellini's lodgings and cooked for him.
Cellini entertained several friends at supper the evening he arrived at his house.
Cellini always kept a lamp by him at night.
Cellini had a safe-conduct document.
Cellini was attacked by the Bargello and his guard at his house.
Cellini defended himself with a dagger when the Bargello's men tried to arrest him.
Cellini presented his safe-conduct to the Bargello, who eventually left without arresting him.
Cellini felt agitated after the encounter with the Bargello and could not sleep.
Cellini intended to let blood as soon as day broke after the Bargello incident.
Cellini consulted with Messer Gaddi about his condition after the Bargello incident.
Cellini's doctor was an empty fribbler who kept perpetually laughing.
Cellini's apprentice Cencio was a youth of superlative talent, bravery, and extreme personal beauty.
Cellini had blue silk and stuff cut for new clothes for himself and Cencio.
Cellini intended to walk in procession after four days at the feast of Our Lady.
Cellini went to see the Pope after the Bargello incident.
The Pope told Cellini to speak with Messer Ambruogio about executing a large piece of golden plate.
Messer Ambruogio had been in concert with Cellini's enemies to bring him back to Rome.
Cellini was unwilling to go to prison as was customary for those receiving a pardon at the feast of Our Lady.
The Pope granted Cellini a dispensation to avoid prison.
Cellini walked in procession between two gentlemen on the appointed day and received his grace.
Cellini was attacked with violent fever attended by extreme cold four days after the procession.
Cellini believed his illness was caused by the encounter with the Bargello.
Francesco da Norcia was a physician of great age and the best repute in Rome.
Cellini's physicians despaired of his life after eight days of illness.
Maestro Francesco said he would rather save Cellini than any of the cardinals in Rome.
Messer Giovanni Gaddi visited Cellini two or three times daily during his illness.
Messer Giovanni Gaddi examined Cellini's handsome fowling-pieces, coats of mail, swords, models, and other possessions during visits.
Matio Franzesi appeared to be waiting impatiently for Cellini's death.
Felice, Cellini's partner, rendered the greatest services during Cellini's illness.
Cellini's brain remained clear and strong despite his physical debilitation.
Cellini experienced a vision of a terrible old man who appeared to drag him toward a huge boat.
Cellini called out to Felice to chase away the old man in his vision.
Felice loved Cellini most affectionately and wept during his illness.
Messer Giovanni Gaddi believed Cellini was delirious when he spoke of the old man.
Mattio Franzesi attributed Cellini's vision to his reading of Dante.
Cellini asked Messer Giovanni Gaddi to remove Mattio Franzesi from his presence.
Messer Giovanni Gaddi sent for Messer Antonio Allegretti, Messer Ludovico, and Messer Annibal Caro to visit Cellini.
Cellini was greatly comforted by the arrival of these worthy men.
Cellini frequently urged Felice to drive away the old man during his illness.
Cellini fell into a terrible swoon when the old man dragged him toward the boat.
During his swoon, Cellini cast insults at Messer Giovanni Gaddi, accusing him of coming to rob him.
Cellini lay still like one dead for more than an hour after his swoon.
Mattio Franzesi wrote to Messer Benedetto Varchi in Florence that Cellini had died.
Messer Benedetto Varchi was Cellini's very dear friend.
Messer Benedetto Varchi composed an admirable sonnet upon Cellini's supposed death.
Cellini remained unconscious for more than three long hours.
Felice used all the remedies prescribed by Maestro Francesco during Cellini's unconsciousness.
Felice ran to Maestro Francesco's door and knocked loudly to wake him.
Maestro Francesco was a very choleric man.
Maestro Francesco gave Felice an oil to anoint Cellini's pulses and heart.
Maestro Francesco instructed Felice to pinch Cellini's little fingers and toes very tightly.
Cellini nearly died from a serious illness.
Felice was devoted to Cellini and stayed by his side during his illness.
Cellini regained consciousness and called out to Felice.
Cellini possessed approximately eight hundred crowns in gold, silver, jewels, and cash.
Cellini willed his money to his poor sister in Florence called Mona Liperata.
Cellini left his property, armour, and fifty golden ducats to Felice.
Messer Giovanni Gaddi felt ashamed and did not visit Cellini again after his illness.
Cellini's brother-in-law arrived from Florence for the inheritance.
Cellini's brother-in-law rejoiced to find him alive.
Maestro Francesco visited Cellini four or five times a day.
Maestro Francesco da Norcia was Cellini's physician.
Cellini was tormented by thirst during his illness.
Cellini had abstained from drinking for many days according to doctors' orders.
Cellini's lad Cencio was far more ill than Cellini and near death's door.
Beatrice was a Pistojan servant-maid in Cellini's household.
Beatrice had stolen certain things of importance from Cellini.
Cellini drank more than a flask full of water.
Cellini sweated and fell into a deep sleep after drinking the water.
Felice beat Beatrice with a stick when he learned she had given Cellini water.
Cellini's health improved after drinking the water.
Maestro Bernardino was a physician who had refused to bleed Cellini at the beginning of his illness.
Maestro Francesco criticized Maestro Bernardino for not understanding the importance of bloodletting.
Cardinal Cornaro had Cellini transported to a place on Monte Cavallo.
Cellini vomited a hairy worm about a quarter of a cubit in length.
The worm had long hairs and was speckled with green, black, and red colors.
Maestro Francesco said Cellini's malady had been so grave that extreme unction might not have been administered in time.
Maestro Francesco asked Cellini to make him a Madonna with his own hand.
Cellini suffered for more than fifty days.
Cellini and Felice traveled to Florence in a pair of baskets.
Cellini's sister wept and laughed over him when he reached her house.
Pier Landi was the best and dearest friend Cellini ever had.
Niccolò da Monte Aguto was a very great friend of Cellini.
Duke Alessandro swore that Cellini was thrusting his head into a halter.
Giorgetto Vassellario of Arezzo was a painter who had turned Cellini's house upside down.
Giorgetto Vassellario was subject to a species of dry scab which he constantly scratched.
Giorgetto Vassellario tore the skin from Manno's leg with his filthy claws.
Giorgetto Vassellario never cut his nails.
Cellini harbored Giorgetto Vassellario in Rome and provided for his costs.
Cellini got Giorgetto into Cardinal de' Medici's household.
Giorgetto told Duke Alessandro that Cellini had abused his Excellency.
Giorgetto claimed Cellini had bragged he meant to be the first to leap upon the walls of Florence with the exiles.
Ottaviano de' Medici put words into Vasari's lips to revenge himself on the Duke.
Ottaviano de' Medici wanted to revenge himself for the Duke's irritation regarding the coinage and Cellini's departure from Florence.
Cellini felt no fear because he was innocent of the crime falsely ascribed to him.
Francesco da Monte Varchi was a physician who attended to Cellini's cure with great skill.
Luca Martini was Cellini's very dear friend.
Luca Martini passed the larger portion of the day with Cellini.
Cellini sent Felice back to Rome to look after their business there.
After fifteen days, Cellini could raise his head from the bolster.
Cellini was unable to walk upon his feet after fifteen days of recovery.
Cellini had himself carried to the palace of the Medici.
Cellini was placed upon the little upper terrace at the Medici palace.
Many of Cellini's friends at court expressed surprise at his visit while so ill.
Cellini had the look of a dead man according to those who saw him.
Cellini publicly stated that a wicked scoundrel had told the Duke he bragged about scaling the walls.
Cellini was carried to the palace of the Medici after being ill for fifteen days.
Cellini had been told by someone that he was dead.
Someone had told the Duke that Cellini bragged he meant to be the first to scale the Duke's walls.
Someone had told the Duke that Cellini had abused him personally.
Giorgio the painter was accused by Maestro Agostino of having accused Cellini.
Giorgio denied that he had accused Cellini.
The Duke told Cellini to be an honest man and regain his health.
Niccolò da Monte Aguto told Cellini he had escaped a dreadful peril.
Niccolò da Monte Aguto warned Cellini that his jeopardy came from Ottaviano de' Medici.
Cellini gave Pietro Pagolo advice about stamping coins.
Cellini went to Rome without saying a word to the Duke or anybody else.
Cellini finished the Duke's medal head in steel in a few days.
Cellini believed the medal head was the finest work of its kind he had ever produced.
Messer Francesco Soderini visited Cellini frequently while he was working on the medal.
Messer Francesco Soderini called Cellini a barbarous wretch for immortalizing the Duke.
Messer Francesco Soderini said the Pope and the Duke had twice considered hanging Cellini without fault.
It was believed that Duke Alessandro was the son of Pope Clement.
Messer Francesco Soderini said he would have robbed Cellini of the medal dies if he could.
Cellini requested that Lorenzino send him the reverse of the medal.
Niccolò da Monte Aguto wrote that Lorenzino was thinking night and day about finishing the reverse.
Cellini composed a design for the reverse of the medal himself.
Cellini was not yet recovered from his terrible illness.
Cellini went on fowling expeditions with his friend Felice.
Felice had no skill in Cellini's art.
Felice's name was Felice Guadagni.
Cellini and Felice were perpetually together day and night.
Cellini and Felice often laughed about the credit Felice had gained.
On Epiphany, Cellini and Felice were near La Magliana.
Cellini shot a good number of ducks and geese that day.
Cellini's dog was named Barucco.
Cellini brought down two geese with a single ball at very long range.
Cellini usually shot with only one ball.
Cellini was usually able to hit his mark at two hundred cubits.
One of the geese was almost dead and the other was badly wounded.
Cellini's dog retrieved one goose and brought it to him.
Cellini's boot filled with water when he stepped into the oozy ground.
Cellini felt his leg freeze on the way back to Rome.
Felice built a fire to help warm Cellini's leg.
Cellini filled his boot with goose feathers instead of using the fire.
Cellini and Felice saw a huge beam of fire over Florence.
Cellini predicted that something of vast importance had happened in Florence.
Cellini's horse ran up a heap of plaster and broken tiles in the road.
Cellini's horse turned a complete somersault.
Cellini escaped unhurt from the accident.
Cellini ran home laughing after the accident.
The next day, news came to Rome of Duke Alessandro's death.
Francesco Soderini said Lorenzino de' Medici had killed the Duke.
Francesco Soderini jeered Cellini about immortalizing the dukes.
Baccio Bettini also bantered Cellini about dukes.
Cellini said he was a poor goldsmith who serves whoever pays him.
Cellini predicted another duke would come within two or three days.
Cosimo de' Medici, the son of Signor Giovanni, was made Duke.
Cellini compared Cosimo to a young man on a mettlesome horse with spurs and bridle.
Cellini said laws cannot be imposed on the master of the law.
The Emperor returned victorious from his expedition against Tunis.
The Pope sent for Cellini to advise on a present for the Emperor.
Cellini suggested presenting the Emperor with a golden crucifix.
Cellini had almost finished an ornament for the crucifix.
Cellini had made three little figures of gold representing Faith, Hope, and Charity.
Cellini carried the crucifix design to the Pope with Christ in wax.
The Pope ordered Messer Latino Juvenale to pay Cellini money the next morning.
Messer Latino Juvenale altered the Pope's arrangement without authorization.
Cellini told Messer Latino Juvenale that neither he nor the Pope could think of anything better than a piece with Christ.
Messer Latino Juvenale tried to get the work given to another goldsmith.
The Pope refused to give the work to another goldsmith.
The Pope wanted to use a Book of Hours of Our Lady as a present for the Empress instead.
The Book of Hours had cost the Cardinal de' Medici more than two thousand crowns.
The Pope wanted the Book of Hours enclosed in a case of massive gold.
The jewels for the case were valued at about six thousand crowns.
Cellini brought the work to great beauty in a few days.
The Pope showed Cellini distinguished signs of favour.
The Pope conceded that Messer Latino Juvenale should have nothing more to do with Cellini.
The Emperor arrived in Rome with extraordinary pomp.
The Emperor gave the Pope a diamond he had bought for twelve thousand crowns.
The Pope ordered Cellini to make a ring from the diamond to the measure of his finger.
The Emperor was expected in Rome in about a month and a half.
The Pope wanted the book to be enclosed in a case of massive gold, richly worked, and adorned with jewels valued at about six thousand crowns.
Cellini brought the book to such beauty in a few days that the Pope was astonished.
The Pope showed Cellini the most distinguished signs of favour.
The Pope conceded that Juvenale should have nothing more to do with Cellini.
The Emperor arrived and numerous triumphal arches of great magnificence were erected in his honour.
The Emperor gave the Pope a diamond which he had bought for twelve thousand crowns.
The Pope ordered Cellini to make a ring to the measure of his holiness' finger.
Cellini suggested that his indisposition would furnish a sound excuse for the unfinished condition of his work.
The Pope approved of Cellini's suggestion.
Cellini said he had the courage to speak with far greater ease and freedom to the Emperor than to the Pope.
Cellini felt more awe addressing the Pope than the Emperor because of the Pope's ecclesiastical adornments and venerable age.
The Pope told Cellini: 'Go, my Benvenuto; you are a man of ability; do us honour, and it will be well for you.'
The Pope ordered out two Turkish horses which had belonged to Pope Clement.
Messer Durante delivered his speech with so much of Brescian lingo that the Emperor could not refrain from smiling at him.
Cellini presented the book to the Emperor and offered himself to complete the work.
The Emperor responded that the book and Cellini were acceptable to him.
The Emperor called Cellini by his name during their conversation.
The Emperor had seen the fastening of Pope Clement's cope on which Cellini had wrought.
Cellini and the Emperor talked for a whole half hour on divers topics artistic and agreeable.
The Emperor ordered five hundred golden crowns to be given to Cellini.
Messer Durante robbed Cellini of his five hundred crowns.
Cellini complained to the Pope about losing the crowns.
The Pope told Cellini he knew how well Cellini had conducted himself and that Cellini should certainly obtain his share of the money.
Four jewellers of Rome were sent to consult with Cellini about setting the diamond.
The diamond had been set by Maestro Miliano Targhetta, the first jeweller of the world in Venice.
Gaio was a man of Milan who was the most presumptuous donkey in the world.
Cellini succeeded in making a foil that was quite as good as Miliano's and exceeded it by far.
Cellini produced a foil by new processes which was a long way better than what he had previously made.
Raffael del Moro said to Gaio that Benvenuto had outdone the foil of Miliano.
Gaio said the diamond was worth two thousand ducats more with Cellini's foil than with Miliano's.
Cellini tinted the diamond anew unseen by the jewellers and showed it to them again.
Gaio said the stone was worth upwards of eighteen thousand crowns with Cellini's new foil, whereas they had valued it at barely twelve thousand.
The other jewellers said Benvenuto is the glory of their art.
Gaio said he would procure for the Pope one thousand golden crowns for the setting of this diamond.
The Pope sent three times on that day to see if the ring was finished.
Cellini took the ring to the palace at twenty-three o'clock.
The Pope was in private audience with the Marchese del Guasto when Cellini arrived.
The Pope asked Cellini to begin some conversation on an important subject to distract from the Marquis.
Cellini discoursed with the Pope upon the methods he had used to tint the stone for near upon an hour.
The Marquis grew tired of waiting and went off fuming.
The Pope bestowed on Cellini the most familiar caresses and exclaimed he would give him a better reward than the thousand crowns.
Latino Juvenale reported to the Pope that Cellini had said disrespectful things about Pope Clement and the current Pope.
Cellini denied having uttered the words attributed to him by Juvenale.
The Pope harboured a deep vindictive feeling against Cellini after hearing Juvenale's report.
Cellini no longer had the same easy access to the Pope's apartments as formerly.
Cellini found the greatest difficulty in procuring audience with the Pope.
Cellini conceived that someone had done him a bad turn.
Cellini was told the whole story but not the name of his calumniator.
Cellini could not imagine who the man was who had slandered him.
Cellini finished the book and took it to the Pope.
The Pope commended the book greatly.
Cellini begged the Pope to send him with the book to the Emperor.
The Pope replied that he would do what he thought fit and that Cellini had performed his part of the business.
The Pope gave orders that Cellini should be well paid.
The two pieces of work on which Cellini spent upwards of two months brought him in five hundred crowns.
Cellini was paid one hundred and fifty crowns for the diamond.
The cover of the book was worth more than a thousand crowns.
Cellini made his mind up to leave Rome without permission.
The Pope sent Cellini's book to the Emperor by the hand of his grandson Signor Sforza.
The Emperor expressed great satisfaction upon accepting the book.
The Emperor immediately asked for Cellini.
Young Signor Sforza said that Cellini had been prevented by illness from coming.
Cellini made preparations for the journey into France.
Cellini wished to go to France alone but was unable on account of a lad in his service called Ascanio.
Ascanio was of very tender age and the most admirable servant in the world.
Ascanio had left a former master named Francesco, a Spaniard and a goldsmith.
Cellini did not much like to take Ascanio lest he should get into a quarrel with the Spaniard.
Ascanio contrived that his master should write Cellini a note informing him that he was free to take him.
Ascanio had been with Cellini some months.
Ascanio came to Cellini both thin and pale of face.
Cellini and others called Ascanio 'the little old man.'
Ascanio affirmed his age to be thirteen years.
Ascanio improved in person during those few months and became the handsomest youth in Rome.
Ascanio showed marvellous aptitude for the art of goldsmithing.
Cellini felt a warm and fatherly affection for Ascanio.
Cellini kept Ascanio clothed as if he had been his own son.
Cellini did not want to have Ascanio initially for fear of offending his master.
Ascanio's master wrote a note informing Cellini that he was free to take Ascanio.
Ascanio had been with Cellini for some months.
Ascanio came to Cellini thin and pale of face.
Ascanio claimed to be thirteen years old.
Ascanio improved in person during those few months with Cellini.
Ascanio became the handsomest youth in Rome.
Ascanio showed marvellous aptitude for Cellini's art.
Ascanio frequently went to thank his former master.
Ascanio's former master had a handsome young woman as his wife named Madonna Francesca.
Madonna Francesca had no reputation for chastity.
Ascanio began to visit Madonna Francesca more frequently than his wont.
Ascanio beat one of Cellini's little shopboys without cause.
Cellini gave Ascanio a severe drubbing with both fists and feet.
Ascanio ran away without cape or cap after the beating.
Cellini did not know where Ascanio was for two days and took no care to find him.
A Spanish gentleman called Don Diego came to speak to Cellini about Ascanio.
Don Diego was the most generous man in the world.
Cellini had made things for Don Diego, which had brought them well acquainted.
Ascanio had gone back to his old master Francesco.
Cellini said Francesco had behaved badly and like a low-bred fellow.
Cellini resolved that Francesco should not have Ascanio.
Cellini threatened to set eyes upon the lad in Francesco's house.
Cellini saw Ascanio working at some trifles in wire at his master's side.
Ascanio bowed to Cellini when he was passing.
Francesco almost laughed Cellini in the face.
Francesco asked through Don Diego whether Cellini would give Ascanio back the clothes.
Francesco said Ascanio should not want for clothes.
Cellini told Don Diego that Francesco was a worthless and dishonoured renegade.
Cellini threatened to kill Francesco if he did not bring Ascanio to his shop before the bell for vespers.
Cellini threatened to treat Ascanio the same way if he did not quit Francesco's house at the appointed hour.
Don Diego inspired such terror in Francesco that he knew not what to do with himself.
Ascanio went to find his father, who had come to Rome from Tagliacozzo.
Ascanio's father advised Francesco to bring Ascanio back to Cellini.
Francesco told Ascanio to go on his own account, and his father should go with him.
Don Diego came with Francesco and Ascanio to Cellini's shop.
Cellini was pacing up and down the shop waiting for the bell to vespers.
Cellini's mind was made up to do one of the bloodiest deeds he had ever attempted.
Francesco was pale as death when he arrived.
Francesco said he had kept Ascanio not thinking that he should offend Cellini.
Ascanio humbly asked for pardon.
Cellini asked Ascanio if he had come to work out the time he promised.
Ascanio answered yes and said he meant never to leave Cellini.
Cellini told the shopboy to hand Ascanio the bundle of clothes.
Cellini gave Ascanio his discharge.
Don Diego stood astonished at Cellini's action.
Ascanio and his father besought Cellini to pardon and take him back.
Cellini asked who spoke for Ascanio.
Ascanio said it was his father.
Cellini agreed to take Ascanio back for the sake of his father.
Cellini had formed the resolution to go toward France.
Cellini saw that the Pope did not hold him in the same esteem as formerly.
Cellini's faithful service had been besmirched by lying tongues.
Cellini feared those who had the power might play him some worse trick.
Cellini was determined to seek better fortune in a foreign land.
Cellini wished to leave Rome without company or license.
Cellini told his faithful friend Felice to make free use of all his effects during his absence.
Cellini left everything he possessed to Felice in case of not returning.
A Perugian workman was in Cellini's employ.
The Perugian workman had helped Cellini on commissions from the Pope.
Cellini paid the Perugian workman's wages and told him he must leave his service.
The Perugian workman begged Cellini to let him go with him at his own charges.
The Perugian workman said it would be better for Cellini to have Italians by him if he stopped to work for the King of France.
Cellini was persuaded to take the Perugian workman on the journey.
Ascanio was present at the debate about the Perugian workman.
Ascanio said half in tears that he wished to remain with Cellini his lifetime.
Cellini told Ascanio that nothing in the world would make him consent.
Cellini saw that Ascanio was preparing to follow on foot.
Cellini engaged a horse for Ascanio.
Cellini put a small valise upon the crupper of Ascanio's horse.
Cellini loaded himself with far more useless baggage than he should otherwise have taken.
Cellini travelled from Rome to Florence, from Florence to Bologna, from Bologna to Venice, and from Venice to Padua.
Cellini's dear friend Albertaccio del Bene made him leave the inn for his house in Padua.
Cellini went to kiss the hand of Messer Pietro Bembo in Padua.
Messer Pietro Bembo was not yet a Cardinal at that time.
Messer Pietro Bembo received Cellini with marks of the warmest affection.
Messer Pietro Bembo told Albertaccio that he wanted Cellini to stay with him.
Cellini spent a very pleasant visit at the house of Messer Pietro Bembo.
Messer Pietro Bembo had a room prepared for Cellini which would have been too grand for a cardinal.
Messer Pietro Bembo always insisted on Cellini taking his meals beside him.
Messer Pietro Bembo hinted in very modest terms that he should greatly like Cellini to take his portrait.
Cellini desired nothing in the world more than to take Bembo's portrait.
Cellini prepared some snow-white plaster in a little box.
Cellini spent two hours on the first day modelling Bembo's portrait.
Cellini blocked out Bembo's head with so much grace of manner that his lordship was fairly astounded.
Messer Pietro Bembo was a man of profound erudition and without a rival in poetry.
Messer Pietro Bembo understood nothing at all about Cellini's art.
Bembo thought Cellini had finished the portrait when he had hardly begun.
Bembo could not comprehend what a long time it took to execute a thing thoroughly.
Bembo wore his beard short after the Venetian fashion.
Cellini had great trouble in modelling a head to his own satisfaction because of Bembo's short beard.
Cellini finished the portrait and judged it about the finest specimen he had produced.
Cellini employed two hundred hours on the wax model.
Bembo conceived that Cellini should have completed the waxen model in two hours and the steel in ten.
Cellini was begging for leave to pursue his journey toward France.
Bembo implored Cellini to design the reverse for his medal.
The reverse for Bembo's medal was to be a Pegasus encircled with a wreath of myrtle.
Cellini performed the task of designing the reverse in the space of some three hours.
Cellini gave the reverse a fine air of elegance.
Bembo was exceedingly delighted with the reverse design.
Bembo said the horse seemed ten times more difficult to do than the portrait.
Bembo kept entreating Cellini to execute the piece in steel.
Cellini told Bembo he was not willing to make it in Padua but promised to take it in hand wherever he might stop to work.
Cellini went to bargain for three horses for his travels.
Bembo took care that a secret watch should be kept over Cellini's proceedings.
Bembo had vast authority in Padua.
The owner of the horses offered to present them to Cellini.
Cellini replied that it was not the horse owner who gave them but the generous donor.
Cellini said he could not accept the horses from the generous donor because he had been unable to present him with any specimen of his craft.
The horse owner said if Cellini did not take the horses, he should get no other horses in Padua.
The horse owner said Cellini should have to make his journey on foot.
Cellini returned to Messer Pietro Bembo.
Bembo affected to be ignorant of the affair with the horses.
Bembo begged Cellini with marks of kindness to remain in Padua.
Cellini had quite resolved to set out.
Cellini had to accept the three horses.
Cellini began his journey with the three horses.
Cellini left Rome on April 1, 1537.
Cellini chose the route through the Grisons.
All other passes were unsafe on account of war.
Cellini crossed the mountains of the Alba and Berlina.
It was the 8th of May when Cellini crossed the mountains.
The snow upon the mountains lay in masses.
Cellini and his party succeeded in surmounting the two Alpine ridges at the utmost hazard of their lives.
Cellini and his party stopped at a place called Valdista.
A Florentine courier named Busbacca arrived at nightfall at Valdista.
Cellini had heard Busbacca mentioned as a man of character and able in his profession.
Cellini did not know that Busbacca had forfeited his reputation by his rogueries.
Busbacca addressed Cellini by his name.
Busbacca said he was going on business of importance to Lyons.
Busbacca entreated Cellini to lend him money for the journey.
Cellini said he had no money to lend.
Cellini offered to pay Busbacca's expenses as far as Lyons if he liked to join him.
Busbacca wept and wheedled Cellini with a long story.
Busbacca said he was carrying things of the utmost importance from Messer Filippo Strozzi.
Busbacca showed Cellini a leather case for a cup.
Busbacca whispered that the case held a goblet of silver which contained jewels to the value of many thousands of ducats.
Busbacca said the case contained letters of vast consequence sent by Messer Filippo Strozzi.
Cellini told Busbacca he ought to let him conceal the jewels about his own person.
Cellini said it would be much less dangerous than carrying them in the goblet.
Cellini offered to supply Busbacca with twenty-five crowns on the security of the goblet.
Cellini estimated the goblet's value at about ten crowns.
Busbacca replied that he would go with Cellini since he could not do otherwise.
Busbacca said giving up the goblet would not be to his honour.
Cellini and Busbacca struck the bargain.
Cellini and his party took horse next morning.
Cellini and his party came to a lake between Valdistate and Vessa.
The lake was fifteen miles long when one reaches Vessa.
Cellini took fright upon beholding the boats upon the lake.
The boats were of pine, of no great size and no great thickness.
The boats were loosely put together and not even pitched.
Four German gentlemen with their four horses were embarking in one of the same sort as Cellini's boat.
Cellini would never have set his foot in the boat if he had not seen the four German gentlemen embarking.
Cellini would far more likely have turned tail.
Cellini took it into his head that those German waters would not drown folk as ours do in Italy.
Cellini's two young men said to him that it was surely dangerous to embark in the craft with four horses.
Cellini replied that his companions were cowards.
Cellini observed that the four gentlemen had taken boat before them and were going on their way with laughter.
Cellini said if the water were wine, he would say they were going gladly to drown themselves in it.
Cellini said as it was but water, he knew well that they had no more pleasure than they had in drowning there.
The lake was fifteen miles long and about three broad.
On one side of the lake rose a mountain very tall and cavernous.
On the other side of the lake was some flat land and grassy.
When Cellini and his party had gone about four miles, it began to storm upon the lake.
The oarsmen asked Cellini and his party to help in rowing.
Cellini and his party helped the oarsmen in rowing awhile.
Cellini made gestures and directed the oarsmen to land them on the farther shore.
The oarsmen said it was not possible because there was not depth of water for the boat.
The oarsmen said there were shoals there which would make the boat go to pieces and drown them all.
The oarsmen kept on urging Cellini and his party to help them.
The boatmen shouted one to the other calling for assistance.
Cellini's horse was an intelligent animal.
Cellini arranged the bridle on his horse's neck and took the end of the halter with his left hand.
Cellini traveled on a lake that was fifteen miles long and about three miles broad.
A mountain rose on one side of the lake and flat, grassy land on the other side.
A storm began on the lake after Cellini's party had gone about four miles.
Cellini directed the oarsmen to land on the farther shore during the storm.
Cellini had an intelligent horse that he used to help escape the dangerous situation on the lake.
Cellini threatened to kill Ascanio with his dagger if Ascanio tried to jump on him during the peril.
Cellini and his party traveled several miles in great peril of their lives on the lake.
Cellini's party encountered four German gentlemen who had already come to shore.
Cellini forced the boatmen to put his party on shore by drawing swords.
Cellini was completely clothed in mail with big boots and carried a gun.
It was raining heavily during the mountain climb.
The German gentlemen accomplished feats of agility leading their horses up the mountain.
Ascanio's horse, a Hungarian breed, made a false step and was impaled on a lance carried by the courier Busbacca.
Another of Cellini's workmen's black horse slipped toward the lake while carrying saddle-bags with Cellini's money and valuables.
Cellini told the young man to save his own life and let the horse go to the devil.
The fall from where the horse slipped was more than a mile of precipitous descent.
Cellini carried only a few hundred crowns in the saddle-bags.
Busbacca had tied jewels worth several thousands of ducats to the horse's saddle-bow.
Busbacca confessed that his case was full of caviare, not jewels as he had claimed.
The horse extricated itself by great effort and did not fall into the lake.
Wild herbs were applied to the wounded horse's throat wound.
Cellini was told the horse would recover if the wound was kept plugged with leaves.
Cellini's party reached their lodging in the wilderness wet, tired, and famished.
Cellini's party traveled to a town beyond Vessa where they passed the night.
A watchman in that town sang through all the hours to warn against fire.
The houses in that town were built of pine wood.
Busbacca's nerves were shaken by the day's adventures and he cried out in his sleep.
Busbacca got drunk in the evening and cried out about drowning, burning, and being in hell.
Cellini's party dined in a place called Lacca and obtained excellent entertainment.
A guide leading Cellini's party slipped and fell with his horse into the water near a dyked lake bank.
The guide began to sing again after falling into the water as if nothing had happened.
Cellini broke away to the right through hedges to avoid the guide's path.
The guide warned in German that Cellini would be put to death if seen by local folk.
Cellini's party arrived at Surich, which Cellini describes as a marvellous city, bright and polished like a little gem.
Cellini's party rested a whole day in Surich.
Cellini's party traveled from Surich to Solutorno, then to Usanna, Geneva, and Lyons.
Cellini traveled always singing and laughing during this journey.
Cellini rested four days in Lyons and had pleasant intercourse with friends there.
Cellini was repaid what he had spent upon Busbacca in Lyons.
A band of venturers tried to murder Cellini's party at Palissa.
Cellini escaped from the venturers through great courage and address.
Cellini traveled from Palissa to Paris without trouble, always singing and laughing.
Cellini went to visit the painter Rosso in Paris after taking repose.
Cellini had done Rosso great benefits in Rome.
Rosso spoke ill of Raffaello da Urbino's works in Rome.
Pupils of Raffaello were resolved to murder Rosso because of his criticism.
Cellini saved Rosso from this peril by keeping close watch on him day and night.
Rosso spoke evil things against San Gallo, the architect.
San Gallo used his influence against Rosso, causing him to lose work.
Cellini lent Rosso some scores of crowns to live upon out of pity.
Cellini expected Rosso to discharge his debt and assist him in the King's service.
When Rosso saw Cellini, his countenance changed suddenly.
Rosso told Cellini that all men's thoughts were occupied with war, not with their profession.
Cellini replied he had brought money enough to return to Rome as he had come to Paris.
Cellini showed Rosso a letter of exchange for five hundred crowns upon Ricciardo del Bene.
Rosso became ashamed and wanted to detain Cellini almost by force.
Cellini took his leave and lodged with a painter named Sguazzella, a Florentine.
Cellini lodged with three horses and three servants at Sguazzella's house.
Cellini sought audience of the King through the introduction of his treasurer, Messer Giuliano Buonaccorti.
Rosso made strenuous exertions against Cellini's admission to the King.
Messer Giuliano brought Cellini to Fontana Bilio and into the presence of the King.
The King granted Cellini a whole hour of very gracious audience.
The King was on the point of setting out for Lyons.
The King told Messer Giuliano to take Cellini with him on the journey.
Cellini entered into close relations with the Cardinal of Ferrara during the journey.
The Cardinal of Ferrara had not yet obtained the hat at that time.
Cellini held long conversations with the Cardinal every evening.
The Cardinal advised Cellini to remain at an abbey of his in Lyons.
The Cardinal was going on to Grenoble.
Cellini was already ill when the party reached Lyons.
Ascanio had taken a quartan fever.
The French and their court had grown irksome to Cellini.
Cellini counted the hours until he could return to Rome.
The Cardinal gave Cellini money sufficient for making a silver bason and jug.
Cellini and his party took good horses and set their faces toward Rome.
Cellini's party passed the Simplon mountains.
Cellini traveled in the company of certain Frenchmen.
Ascanio was troubled by his quartan fever during the return journey.
Cellini had a slow fever he could not throw off.
Cellini's stomach was out of order for four months.
Cellini hardly ate one whole loaf of bread in a week during this period.
Cellini longed to reach Italy, desiring to die there rather than in France.
Cellini's party came to a river near a place called Indevedro after crossing the Simplon mountains.
The river was broad and very deep, spanned by a long narrow bridge without ramparts.
A thick white frost had fallen that morning.
Cellini recognized the bridge was dangerous and bade his servants and young men dismount and lead their horses.
Cellini got across the bridge without accident.
One of the Frenchmen was a gentleman and the other was a scrivener.
The scrivener jeered at Cellini and the French gentleman for being frightened.
The scrivener cried out in French that Cellini was a man of poor spirit.
The scrivener's horse suddenly slipped over the bridge.
The scrivener and his horse plunged into a deep wide pool.
Both the scrivener and horse went down below the water.
Cellini ran at full speed and scrambled onto a rock.
Cellini dangled over the rock and seized the scrivener's gown skirt to pull him up.
The scrivener had drunk his bellyful of water and was within an ace of being drowned.
The scrivener answered Cellini in French that the important things to save were his writings worth many scores of crowns.
The scrivener seemed to say this in anger while dripping wet and spluttering.
Cellini ordered the guides to help the scrivener and said he would see them paid.
One guide picked up all the scrivener's writings with great address and trouble.
The scrivener lost not one of his writings.
The other guide refused to trouble himself by rendering assistance.
Cellini rescued a scrivener from drowning in a pool.
The scrivener was more concerned about his writings than his life.
Cellini was responsible for managing a common purse and paying guides.
Cellini rewarded the guide who helped rescue the scrivener from his own pocket.
The guide who refused to help demanded payment anyway.
Cellini and his companions were threatened by a crowd armed with a boar-spear.
Cellini was mounted on a horse and carried an arquebuse.
Cellini was prepared to fight and possibly die rather than be insulted.
Cellini parted ways with the Frenchman and remained friendly with the other guide.
Cellini traveled to Ferrara with three horses.
Cellini paid his respects to the Duke of Ferrara.
Cellini had fasted for more than four months.
Cellini declined to eat at the Duke's table and conversed with him instead.
The Duke of Ferrara sent Cellini food from his own banquet.
Cellini traveled from Ferrara to Santa Maria at Loreto and then to Rome.
Cellini had a faithful assistant named Felice in Rome.
Cellini abandoned his old shop to Felice and opened a larger one.
Cellini expected King Francis would not remember him.
Cellini accepted commissions from several noblemen in Rome.
Cellini began work on a bason and jug ordered by Cardinal Ferrara.
Cellini employed a Perugian workman and gave him clothes and money.
The Perugian workman owed Cellini about seventy crowns.
The Perugian workman agreed to pay three crowns monthly.
The Perugian workman abandoned Cellini's shop after two months without paying.
Cellini initially wanted to cut off the workman's arm as punishment.
Cellini's friends persuaded him to seek legal redress instead.
Cellini sued the workman before the auditor of the Camera.
Cellini won his lawsuit and had the workman imprisoned.
Cellini employed eight workmen and worked day and night.
Cellini received a letter from the Cardinal of Ferrara about King Francis wanting his service.
King Francis ordered one thousand golden crowns for Cellini's journey.
Cardinal de' Gaddi falsely claimed he had already sent Cellini money.
Cellini had spoken to Cardinal de' Gaddi only twice in his lifetime.
Cellini believed Cardinal de' Gaddi acted from lightheadedness rather than malice.
Cellini responded to Cardinal Ferrara that he knew nothing of Cardinal de' Gaddi's claims.
Cellini stated he had more work in Rome than ever before.
The Perugian workman accused Cellini of stealing jewels worth eighty thousand ducats.
The workman claimed the jewels belonged to the Church and were stolen from Castel Sant' Angelo.
Cellini was arrested by Crespino, the Bargello, while walking on the Strada Giulia.
Cellini was taken to Castel Sant' Angelo as a prisoner.
Cellini was thirty-seven years old at the time of his first imprisonment.
Cellini had previously assassinated Pompeo at the corner where he was arrested.
Cellini was held in prison for eight days before examination.
Cellini was examined by the Governor of Rome, the Procurator-Fiscal, and a criminal judge.
Cellini was accused of unsetting jewels from papal tiaras, mitres, and rings.
Cellini was accused of sewing jewels into the Pope's clothes.
Cellini was accused of sequestering jewels worth eighty thousand crowns.
Cellini laughed at the accusations against him.
Cellini argued that if the accusation were true, he could not be punished due to suspended law during the sack of Rome.
Cellini suggested he might have kept the jewels as a faithful servant for the Church.
Benvenuto Cellini had come to Rome approximately twenty years before this incident.
Cellini had never been sent to prison in Rome or elsewhere during those twenty years.
Cellini had killed men in Rome.
Cellini exercised his art in matters of vast importance in Rome.
Cellini believed Rome was the seat of Christ and the proper place to seek protection from temporal princes.
Cellini was present when Pope Clement allowed a diamond worth about four thousand crowns to fall from his finger as a gift to Cesare Iscatinaro.
Cellini helped defend the castle when Imperial troops entered the Borgo by operating abandoned guns.
Cellini's comrade Raffaello da Montelupo was a sculptor who initially hid in fear during the Imperial attack.
Cellini and Raffaello da Montelupo together killed many enemies who were attacking the castle.
Cellini shot at Iscatinaro for showing disrespect to Pope Clement.
Cellini wounded the Prince of Orange in the head below the trenches of the castle.
Cellini made ornaments of silver, gold, and jewels for the Holy Church.
Cellini made models and coins for the Holy Church.
Cellini received wounds and stonings during the sack of Rome.
Pope Paolo had promised Cellini some small remuneration.
King Francis of France received news that the Pope was keeping Cellini in prison unjustly.
King Francis sent Monsignor di Morluc as his ambassador to Rome.
King Francis claimed Cellini from the Pope as a man of his Majesty.
The Pope advised King Francis that Cellini was a troublemaker who gave much trouble by fighting.
The Pope claimed he kept Cellini in prison for homicides and other misdeeds.
King Francis responded that he maintained justice excellently in his realm.
King Francis said he rewarded men of parts and virtue and chastised the troublesome.
The Pope's fear that Cellini would tell the world of his mistreatment caused the Pope to seek ways to have Cellini killed.
Messer Giorgio, a Florentine knight of the Ugolini family, was the castellan of Sant' Angelo.
The castellan showed Cellini great courtesy and let him go free about the castle on parole.
The castellan allowed Cellini to work at his trade while imprisoned.
Cellini kept his shop in Rome open while imprisoned in the castle.
Cellini's apprentice Ascanio brought him things to work on at the castle.
Cellini secured the attachment of all the guards and many soldiers of the castle.
Soldiers of the castle told Cellini he ought to escape and offered to aid him.
Cellini refused to escape because he had given his parole to the castellan.
Cellini believed a prisoner is obliged to keep faith with a worthy man.
Cellini preferred to lose his life rather than break his promise to the castellan.
A friar of the Palavisina house was Cellini's companion of misery in prison.
The friar was a very famous preacher.
The friar had been arrested as a Lutheran.
Cellini found the friar to be an excellent companion intellectually but a scoundrel regarding his religion.
Cellini admired the friar's fine intellectual qualities.
Cellini hated the friar's dirty vices and taxed him with them.
The friar argued that Cellini was not bound to observe faith with the castellan since he had become a prisoner.
Cellini responded that as a man he was bound to keep his word under all circumstances.
The friar read Cellini the sermons of Fra Jerolimo Savonarola.
The friar expounded Savonarola's sermons with such lucidity and learning that his comment was finer than the text.
Cellini remained in ecstasies of admiration for the friar's exposition.
The friar asked Cellini what means he would use to open dungeon doors and effect his flight.
Cellini claimed he was quite sure of being able to open the most baffling locks and bars.
Cellini said opening prison locks would be to him like eating a bit of new cheese.
The friar challenged Cellini's boasts, suggesting he would fail if put to proof.
Cellini demonstrated to the friar the facility with which he could open locks.
The castellan allowed Cellini to roam at pleasure over the whole fortress.
The castellan did not lock Cellini in at night, unlike other prisoners.
The castellan allowed Cellini to employ himself with gold, silver, or wax according to his whim.
Cellini laboured several weeks at a bason ordered by Cardinal Ferrara.
The irksomeness of Cellini's imprisonment bred in him a disgust for his work.
Cellini took to modelling in wax some little figures of his fancy for recreation.
The friar stole a piece of wax that Cellini was using.
The friar used the wax to get false keys made based on Cellini's method.
The friar chose a registrar named Luigi, a Paduan, as his accomplice.
Luigi was in the castellan's service.
The locksmith revealed the friar and Luigi's plot to make false keys.
The castellan recognized the wax Cellini was using and understood the escape plot.
The castellan felt betrayed by Cellini despite his kindness to him.
The castellan had Cellini shut up with disagreeable circumstances after discovering the plot.
The castellan's servants called Cellini ungrateful, light, and disloyal.
Cellini maintained that he had never broken faith with the castellan.
Cellini said he would maintain his innocence at the peril of his life.
Cellini had strained his sense of right to show the castellan kindness.
The castellan shut Cellini up with disagreeable circumstances.
The castellan's servants were extremely fond of Cellini.
Cellini was convinced of his innocence regarding the accusations.
Cellini retorted hotly that he had never broken faith.
Cellini said he would maintain his words at the peril of his life.
A servant brought Cellini wax with the model of keys.
Cellini told the castellan the whole course of events.
The castellan arrested a friar based on Cellini's explanation.
The friar betrayed the registrar.
The registrar ran a risk of being hanged.
The castellan hushed the affair up despite it reaching the Pope's ears.
The castellan saved his registrar from the gallows.
The castellan gave Cellini the same freedom as before.
Cellini began to think of escape after seeing how rigorously the affair was prosecuted.
Cellini had new sheets of coarse fabric brought to him.
Cellini told his servants he had given sheets to poor soldiers.
Cellini's servants, especially Felice, kept the secret of the sheets in loyalty.
Cellini emptied a straw mattress and burned its stuffing in his chimney.
Cellini cut strips from sheets one-third of a cubit in breadth.
Cellini believed he had made enough strips to clear the height of the central keep of Sant' Angelo.
Cellini's shop was closed by order of Cardinals Santi Quattro and Cornaro.
The Cardinals told Cellini openly that the Pope would not set him at large.
The Cardinals said King Francis's favors had done Cellini more harm than good.
Monsignor di Morluc conveyed to the King that the Pope should hand Cellini to ordinary judges.
The Pope was irritated by the King's message and decided to keep Cellini a prisoner for life.
The castellan did his utmost to assist Cellini.
Cellini's enemies taunted and reviled those who visited him in prison.
Ascanio visited Cellini twice a day.
Ascanio asked for a jacket cut from Cellini's blue silk vest.
Cellini had only worn the blue silk vest once, in procession.
Cellini refused to give Ascanio the vest.
Ascanio took Cellini's refusal very ill.
Ascanio said he wanted to go home to Tagliacozzo.
Cellini answered in rage that Ascanio could not please him better than by leaving.
Cellini told Ascanio he would never show his face again.
Cellini and Ascanio were walking round the keep of the castle when they had this exchange.
The castellan was also taking the air at the keep.
Cellini begged the castellan to order guards to keep Ascanio out.
Cellini told the castellan that Ascanio came to add to his great trouble.
The castellan was grieved because he knew Ascanio to be a lad of marvellous talents.
The castellan noted that Ascanio was so fair a person that everyone who saw him seemed bound to love him.
Ascanio went away weeping.
Ascanio carried a small scimitar hidden beneath his clothes.
Ascanio met Jeronimo the Perugian and Michele, both goldsmiths and Cellini's chief enemies.
Michele was Jeronimo's friend and Ascanio's enemy.
Michele called out taunting Ascanio about his father in the castle.
Ascanio struck Michele with the scimitar twice on the head.
The first blow felled Michele to earth.
The second blow lopped three fingers off Michele's right hand.
Michele lay there like a dead man.
The matter was reported to the Pope.
The Pope ordered Cellini to be given three days to prepare his defence.
The castellan went to the Pope and informed him that Cellini was no accomplice in the matter.
The castellan told the Pope that Cellini had sent Ascanio about his business.
The castellan's pleading saved Cellini's life from the impending tempest.
Ascanio escaped to Tagliacozzo, his home.
Ascanio wrote begging Cellini's pardon a thousand times.
Ascanio acknowledged his wrong in adding troubles to Cellini's grave disaster.
Ascanio protested that if Cellini came out from prison, he meant never to abandon him.
